Karpov used the QGD throughout his 1975-1985 World Championship reign. The structure (pawns on d5 + e6, knight on f6, bishop on e7, king on g8) hasn't been broken in 150 years — there's literally no opponent prep strong enough to win in the QGD by force. Levy teaches it because it lets Black focus on middlegame plans instead of opening theory. The downside: your opponent will recognise it immediately and might steer for a draw. The upside: you can't lose in 25 moves.
keyIdeas: Defend d5 with e6 immediately — not c6 (that's the Slav, a different opening). The …e6 move locks in your light-squared bishop on c8, which becomes the QGD's biggest practical problem to solve. Plan its escape from move 1.
The c8-bishop is your project. The three main paths: …Bd7-…Be8-…f5/g6 (slowest but safest), …b6+…Bb7 (after preparing with …a6 if needed), or trade it off via …Bg4 after Nf3. Never let it die on c8.

…c5 is the equalising break, not the winning break. After …c5 the position opens, your pieces can move, and you reach a balanced position. But …c5 played too early hangs the d5-pawn; played too late is unnecessary. The right moment is when both knights are out, both rooks are connected, and White hasn't yet played dxc5 themselves.

Against the Exchange Variation (cxd5 exd5), you're playing for the Carlsbad structure — minority attack chess. White will try b4-b5 attacking your c6-pawn; you defend with …a6 and …Rb8 + …b5 yourself, neutralising the attack and aiming for the slightly better endgame.
traps: If White plays the Exchange Variation TOO early (cxd5 before c4 has been developed), Black can answer …exd5 with confidence — the resulting IQP-shaped middlegame has Black with active pieces on c-file, e-file, and the standard …Bf5 + …Nb4-d3 attacking plan. Easier game than the QGD-proper.
warnings: NEVER take on c4 with …dxc4 in the QGD unless you have a concrete plan. Surrendering the central tension lets White play e4 with a strong centre and a tempo against your c4-pawn (White recaptures Bxc4 with development).

The c8-bishop is your problem. If you forget to plan its development, you'll reach move 20 with a piece that can only see two squares. Build every move with one eye on how the light-squared bishop eventually gets out.

variations.name: Classical 5.Bf4 Line
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 Be7 Bf4 O-O e3 c5 dxc5 Bxc5 Qc2 Nc6 a3 Qa5 Rd1 Be7
variations.explanation: The modern QGD mainline against the active 5.Bf4 system. After 5...O-O 6.e3 c5! (yes, …c5 played early!) 7.dxc5 Bxc5 — Black grabs the central pawn back with the bishop, accepting White's slight space advantage but reaching active piece play. 8.Qc2 Nc6 9.a3 Qa5 (eyeing c3 and creating threats against the pinned knight) 10.Rd1 — and now the funny 10...Be7! reroutes the bishop AWAY from the active c5-square back to e7. Why? Because Be7 prepares …Nd7 + …e5 freeing the position. Looks like waste of tempo; actually a typical QGD manoeuvre — pieces reach their best squares slowly, not directly.

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 Nbd7 Nf3 Re8 Qc2 c6 O-O Nf8
variations.explanation: The Exchange Variation transforms the QGD into a Carlsbad structure — Black has a pawn on c6 supporting d5, White has pawns on c5-side and the b-pawn ready to push to b5. After 4.cxd5 exd5 5.Bg5 Be7 6.e3 O-O 7.Bd3 Nbd7 8.Nf3 Re8 9.Qc2 c6 10.O-O, the position settles. The plan is the Minority Attack: White will play Rab1 + b4 + b5 trying to create a weakness on c6 (after bxc6 bxc6 Black has a backward c-pawn). Black's counter: the 10...Nf8! manoeuvre reroutes the knight to g6 or e6 supporting the …f5 break or the …Bf5 light-square trade. Outplay your opponent with patient regrouping.

openings.name: Ponziani Opening (GothamChess)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 c3 Nf6 d4 Nxe4 d5 Bc5 dxc6 Bxf2+ Ke2 bxc6
openings.overview: The Ponziani is the ambush specialist's dream. Named after Italian theoretician Domenico Lorenzo Ponziani, who analyzed the opening in his 1769 chess book — making it one of the oldest named openings in chess; despite this 250-year pedigree, it never took off in serious tournament play and was relegated to footnote status for centuries. Only in the YouTube era have streamers and content creators (Levy among them) revived it as a surprise weapon for amateur play. 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.c3 — and your opponent stares at the board confused. 'What is this? Is this just a slow Italian? Should I play …Nf6? …d5?' That confusion IS the opening. 3.c3 prepares d4 with extra support; if Black answers ANYTHING that doesn't immediately challenge the centre, you crash through with 4.d4 and reach a position no Italian player has prepared.
keyIdeas: c3 + d4 is the entire idea. The Italian Game's c3+d4 break is delayed (you only play it after castling); the Ponziani plays c3 IMMEDIATELY, threatening d4 before Black has finished developing.

Watch for the natural-looking 3…Nf6 — this is what 95% of opponents play. Then 4.d4! and if 4…Nxe4 5.d5! kicks the c6-knight AND attacks the e4-knight (since it's no longer defended by Nc6). Black's pieces tangle and material is lost.

Against the principled 3…d5, your answer is the unusual 4.Qa4! — pinning the c6-knight to the d-pawn. This isn't a queen sortie like the Scandinavian; it's a specific tactical resource that disrupts Black's whole development.
The Ponziani has no theory at the club level — your opponents are playing on intuition while you're playing on preparation. Even if engines call the position equal, the tempo advantage is real.
traps: After 3…Nf6 4.d4 Nxe4?? 5.d5! the c6-knight has to move (no defender), but wherever it goes (5…Ne7 or 5…Nb8), White plays 6.Qa4+ winning the e4-knight. Pure pin-and-fork tactics from move 5.
warnings: Be ready for 3…d5! — the principled equaliser. After 4.Qa4 Nf6 5.Nxe5 Bd6 6.Nxc6 bxc6, Black has the bishop pair AND active piece play. Engines call it equal; if you don't know the line, you'll be on the back foot.

NEVER play d4 without first playing c3. If you skip c3 (or play d4 too soon), Black's 4…exd4 5.Nxd4 reaches an Open Game where the c3-pawn would have been useful — you've simply played a worse Scotch.

variations.name: Main Line 3...Nf6 4.d4
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 c3 Nf6 d4 Nxe4 d5 Bc5 dxc6 Bxf2+ Ke2 bxc6
variations.explanation: The sharpest Ponziani line — Black accepts the gambit and grabs e4 with the knight. White responds with 5.d5 attacking the c6-knight. Black's only real try is 5...Bc5! threatening Bxf2+ with discovered tactics. After 6.dxc6 (winning a pawn) Bxf2+! 7.Ke2 — the king walks into the centre, but it's actually safe there. Why? Because after 7...bxc6 (Black recovers the pawn via the open b-file), the position is theoretically balanced but practically explosive. White has a king on e2 looking exposed but with all pieces ready to develop; Black has a slight material advantage (gained the e4-pawn) but a wrecked structure (doubled c-pawns). The position favours whoever has studied it more. If you've watched Levy's video — that's you.

variations.name: 3...d5 Central Counter
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 c3 d5 Qa4 Nf6 Nxe5 Bd6 Nxc6 bxc6
variations.explanation: The principled refutation — Black challenges the centre immediately. White's reply is the brilliant 4.Qa4! pinning the c6-knight against the e8-king (via the diagonal a4-d7-e8 with the d-pawn on d5 blocking). Black plays 4...Nf6 developing, but White grabs the loose e5-pawn with 5.Nxe5! — possible because c6 is pinned. Black tries 5...Bd6 hitting the knight on e5, but after 6.Nxc6 bxc6, White has won a pawn. The structural cost: Black has doubled c-pawns and the bishop pair. Theoretically balanced (Black has dynamic compensation), but at amateur level the extra pawn often holds.

openings.name: Fantasy Variation vs Caro-Kann (GothamChess)
openings.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 f3 dxe4 fxe4 e5 Nf3 exd4 Bc4 Nf6 O-O Bc5
openings.overview: Levy made the Fantasy Variation famous on YouTube — but it has older roots. The 3.f3 move was experimented with by Hungarian GM Geza Maroczy in the early 1900s as a surprise weapon, and David Bronstein later employed it in his unique, creative style. It remained an obscure sideline until modern theoreticians revived it for the online era. Every Caro-Kann player you face has prepared the Classical (3.Nc3) and the Advance (3.e5). Almost nobody has prepared the Fantasy (3.f3). The numbers don't lie: at 1500-2000 level, the surprise alone produces a +0.4 rating-point swing — that's the difference between a draw and a win. The system works because 3.f3 supports a massive central pawn duo while keeping White's options open: you can play for a positional crush after 3...e6, or accept the gambit-style mess after 3...dxe4 4.fxe4. Either way, you're playing memorised lines and Black is improvising.
keyIdeas: The pawn structure e4-d4-f3 dominates the centre. Don't trade these pawns lightly — the d-pawn is the most important; if Black ever plays …e5 hitting it, support with Nf3 rather than dxe5 (which simplifies and helps Black).

Bc4 is mandatory, NOT Bd3. The c4-bishop on the a2-g8 diagonal is the whole reason f3 makes positional sense — the bishop targets f7, AND the f3-pawn defends the e4-pawn, freeing the bishop from defensive duties.

If Black plays the principled …e5 hitting d4, you HAVE to play Nf3 calmly (not dxe5 which would help Black). After 5.Nf3 exd4 you're a pawn down but you have huge development and the open f-file — full gambit compensation.

Against the solid …e6, the centre stays closed and you play for the kingside attack with Bf4, Qd3 (or Qd2), O-O-O, then g4-g5 like an open Sicilian English Attack. Just remember which side castled — your king on c1 has to be solid before you commit pawns forward.
traps: If Black plays 3…dxe4 4.fxe4 e5?? — the natural-looking central counter — White just plays 5.dxe5! winning a clean pawn. Black's queen has no good square (5...Qxd1+ 6.Kxd1 actually favours White — the e5-pawn cramps Black). Black's only sound reply to 4.fxe4 is …e5 followed by …exd4 (gambit-style), not the simple 4…e5 grab.
After 3…e6 4.Nc3 Bb4 (the natural pin) — your move is 5.Bf4! NOT Bd2. The Bf4 keeps the central tension AND develops to a more active square; after 5...Nf6 6.e5 cramps the f6-knight back.
warnings: f3 only works after 1.e4 c6 2.d4 d5 3.f3 — never play f3 in OTHER Caro-Kann move orders (like the Classical with 2.Nc3 first), where the f3-pawn would just weaken the kingside without preparing a central duo.

Be ready for the …Bg4 pin pattern. The f3-pawn weakens White's defence of the e4-pawn AND blocks the natural Nf3 development; if you ever play Nf3 with the queen still on d1, the …Bg4 pin is real and can lose material to a follow-up …Bxf3.

variations.name: Main Line 3...dxe4 4.fxe4
variations.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 f3 dxe4 fxe4 e5 Nf3 exd4 Bc4 Nf6 O-O Bc5
variations.explanation: Levy's main attacking line — and this one's a real fight. Black accepts the central exchange with 3...dxe4 4.fxe4, then immediately challenges your remaining centre with 4...e5. You defend the d4-pawn with 5.Nf3 (NOT 5.dxe5 which gives Black equality) — accepting that you'll lose a pawn for development. Black grabs with 5...exd4. Now your plan: 6.Bc4 (NOT Bd3 — the c4-bishop is the heart of the system) Nf6 7.O-O Bc5 — both sides develop aggressively. The position now: you're down a pawn, but you have the open f-file (rook on f1 ready), the Bc4 aimed at f7, the e4-pawn cramping Black, and Black hasn't castled. The compensation is real and concrete. Aim for tactics around the f-file and the b3-square if Black plays …b5 hitting the bishop.

variations.name: Solid 3...e6
variations.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 f3 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Bf4 Ne7 Qd3 b6
variations.explanation: Black plays the safe 3...e6 declining the central tension. You respond 4.Nc3 Bb4 (the natural pin) 5.Bf4! — the active bishop development that keeps central tension AND eyes c7 and e5 squares. Black tries to challenge with 5...Ne7 (heading to g6 supporting f5 or trying to trade your bishop) 6.Qd3 b6 — Black fianchettos hoping to put pressure on e4 via …Bb7. The position is a closed centre middlegame; your plan is to castle queenside, play g4, push pawns toward the kingside while Black tries to find counterplay against e4. Slower than the main line but easier to play if you don't like tactical chess.

variations.name: Aggressive 3...g6
variations.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 f3 g6 Nc3 Bg7 Be3 dxe4 fxe4 Nf6 Nf3 O-O Bd3
variations.explanation: Black tries the unusual 3...g6 + …Bg7 fianchetto — a Pirc-style setup. You develop naturally with 4.Nc3 Bg7 5.Be3 (NOT Bf4 here — Be3 supports d4 and prepares queenside castling) dxe4 6.fxe4 (recover the e-pawn) Nf6 7.Nf3 O-O 8.Bd3 — only NOW you can play Bd3, because the e4-pawn is solidly defended by Nc3 + Nf3 + Bd3. Bd3 points at h7 with attacking ideas; the centre e4+d4 holds; Black's fianchetto bishop on g7 bites on the d4-pawn defended by everything. White has a comfortable structural edge.

openings.name: Milner-Barry Gambit vs French (GothamChess)
openings.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 e5 c5 c3 Nc6 Nf3 Qb6 Bd3 cxd4 cxd4 Bd7 O-O Nxd4 Nxd4 Qxd4 Nc3 a6 Qe2 Ne7 Kh1
openings.overview: Here's the deal with the French Defence: if you let Black play it on their terms, they reach a structure they've studied since they were a kid and you spend the next 60 moves chipping at a granite wall. The Milner-Barry says: not today. Named after Stuart Milner-Barry — British chess master AND Bletchley Park codebreaker who worked alongside Alan Turing on cracking the Enigma — this gambit was his pet anti-French weapon in the 1930s-40s. After 1.e4 e6 2.d4 d5 3.e5 (the Advance) c5 4.c3 Nc6 5.Nf3, when Black plays the natural 5...Qb6 hitting d4, you don't defend the pawn — you sacrifice it with 6.Bd3! Black is invited to grab it; if they do, you've traded one pawn for total development supremacy. If they don't, you've reached a position with the better-placed pieces.
keyIdeas: The d4-pawn is the bait. After Black plays …Qb6 hitting it, you respond Bd3 — NOT Nc3 (which blocks the queen's defence) and NOT Qd2 (passive). The point: Bd3 develops AND prepares O-O; if Black takes the d4-pawn, your lead in development is worth more than a pawn.

The standard sequence after Black accepts: 6...cxd4 7.cxd4 Nxd4 8.Nxd4 Qxd4 9.Nc3 — Black's queen is now in the open, the Nc3 develops with tempo, and Black is forced into defensive moves while you finish development.

The e5-pawn is your structural ally for the whole game. It cramps Black's c8-bishop (locked behind …b6 or …f6), it controls f6 and d6, and it supports a future f4-f5 kingside attack. Defend it like a king.

After 9.Nc3, look for Black's natural 9...a6 (preparing …b5) and respond 10.Qe2 + 11.Kh1 — the king tucks away while you prepare Rd1 piling on the queen. Black has the extra pawn but spends the next 15 moves trying to consolidate.
traps: If Black gets really greedy with 9...Qxb2?? — punishment via 10.Nc3 Qb4 (only square — Qa3 hangs to Nb5) 11.Rb1 Qa5 12.Nb5! — the knight forks ideas (Nc7+, Nd6+) and Black's queen is in serious trouble. The b2-pawn is double-poisoned.
warnings: ONLY play this against the Advance French (after 3.e5 — Black has committed to …c5). It does NOT work against the Winawer (3.Nc3 Bb4) or the Classical (3.Nc3 Nf6) — those need different systems entirely.

If Black declines the gambit with 6...Bd7 (instead of 6...cxd4), DON'T try to hold the d-pawn — just develop with 7.O-O and play a normal Advance French. The Milner-Barry's whole point is the gambit; if Black declines, accept the equal middlegame.

variations.name: Main Gambit Line
variations.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 e5 c5 c3 Nc6 Nf3 Qb6 Bd3 cxd4 cxd4 Bd7 O-O Nxd4 Nxd4 Qxd4 Nc3 a6 Qe2 Ne7 Kh1
variations.explanation: The full Milner-Barry. Black accepts the pawn sacrifice with 6...cxd4 7.cxd4 Bd7 (developing first, planning Nxd4 next) 8.O-O Nxd4! (the moment of truth — Black grabs the pawn) 9.Nxd4 Qxd4. White is down a pawn but plays 10.Nc3 with tempo on the queen, 10...a6 (preventing Nb5 fork) 11.Qe2 (preparing Rd1 + Rad1) 11...Ne7 (Black develops) 12.Kh1 — the king safety prophylaxis before the attack begins. Position: Black is up a pawn but undeveloped (rook still on h8, king on e8, bishop on f8); White has every piece in optimal place. The next 5 moves are forced for Black — defend or die — while White picks up tempos building the attack.

variations.name: Black Declines with Bd7
variations.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 e5 c5 c3 Nc6 Nf3 Qb6 Bd3 Bd7 O-O cxd4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nxd4 Qxd4 Nc3 Qb6
variations.explanation: Sometimes Black plays 6...Bd7 first (developing) rather than 6...cxd4 immediately. After 7.O-O cxd4 8.cxd4 Nxd4 9.Nxd4 Qxd4 10.Nc3 — same gambit position reached via different move order. Black's only difference: the bishop is already on d7 (so 10...Qb6 retreating the queen is the standard plan). The end position favours White's better development, but Black has the extra pawn AND has avoided the worst of the Milner-Barry attack by retreating the queen early.

variations.name: Anti-French 3.e5 Advance
variations.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 e5 c5 c3 Nc6 Nf3 Nh6 Bd3 cxd4 cxd4 Nf5 Bxf5 exf5 Nc3 Be6 O-O Be7
variations.explanation: When Black tries the unusual 5...Nh6 (heading to f5 to challenge your light-squared bishop), accept the trade: 6.Bd3 cxd4 7.cxd4 Nf5 8.Bxf5! exf5 — Black has doubled f-pawns and a cramped position. White's central pawns plus open structure for the rooks give a long-term positional edge. Black's …Be6 + …Be7 development is passive; you're playing for the win on positional grounds rather than the usual gambit fireworks.

openings.name: Anti-Sicilian Rossolimo (GothamChess)
openings.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 e6 O-O Nge7 Re1 a6 Bf1 d5 exd5 Nxd5 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nxd4 Qxd4
openings.overview: The Sicilian is the most theoretical opening in chess. The Rossolimo says: 'Don't play that game.' Named after Russian-born American GM Nicolas Rossolimo, who systematized the 3.Bb5 approach in the 1940s-50s and used it throughout his career to score remarkable results against the Sicilian. Magnus Carlsen has played it consistently throughout his career — both as a winning weapon against weaker opposition AND as a peace offering against the best, since the resulting positions reward technical skill over memorization. After 1.e4 c5 2.Nf3 Nc6, White plays 3.Bb5 — pinning the c6-knight against Black's development. Now there's no Open Sicilian. No Najdorf preparation. Black has to play their own game against a setup that 9 out of 10 Sicilian players haven't studied beyond move 7. You'll lose to a 2200; you'll crush a 1500.
keyIdeas: Bb5 is the heart of the system. It pins the c6-knight (preventing standard …e5 pushes), prepares Bxc6 (creating doubled pawns on c6), and develops the bishop to a useful square. Don't trade it lightly — the bishop on b5 is the entire opening.

Decide early: trade or retreat. Against …a6, your two choices are 4.Bxc6 (commit to the structural game — doubled pawns for Black, two bishops for you) or 4.Ba4 (keep the bishop, play for Italian-style positions). Bxc6 is more concrete; Ba4 keeps options open.

The d4 break still works in the Rossolimo — but on YOUR terms. After castling, you can play d4 in one move (one of White's structural advantages over an Open Sicilian where d4 happens on move 3). The IQP positions favour White because the c6-pawn is doubled and weak.

Avoids Najdorf/Dragon/Sveshnikov/Taimanov/Kan completely. The Sicilian player has spent years studying open lines — none of that helps in the Rossolimo. You're playing a different game and they're improvising from move 7.
traps: After 3...g6? (the fianchetto attempt) — Levy's video has 200K views on this. Play 4.Bxc6! bxc6 (or dxc6) and Black's structure is permanently damaged. The dark-squared bishop wanted to go to g7 long-diagonal, but now the diagonal targets nothing because the e5-pawn is gone. Pure structural punishment.
warnings: The Rossolimo ONLY works against 2…Nc6. If Black plays 2…d6 (Najdorf move order) or 2…e6 (Taimanov move order), you can't play Bb5+ because there's nothing to pin. Have a different anti-Sicilian (like the Closed Sicilian with c3 or the Alapin) in reserve.

Don't trade Bxc6 reflexively — sometimes the pin is the point. If Black hasn't played …a6 yet and you have other useful moves (O-O, Re1, d4 prep), use the pin as a long-term asset rather than cashing it in immediately.

variations.name: Rossolimo 3.Bb5 e6
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 e6 O-O Nge7 Re1 a6 Bf1 d5 exd5 Nxd5 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nxd4 Qxd4
variations.explanation: The flagship Rossolimo line. Black plays 3...e6 (preparing …d5 to challenge the centre) and …Nge7 (the knight goes to e7 supporting …d5). You set up with 4.O-O Nge7 5.Re1 a6 — and now the unusual retreat 6.Bf1! The bishop goes back to f1 to keep the b1-h7 diagonal open AND get out of the way of a future d4 push. After 6...d5 (Black's break) 7.exd5 Nxd5 8.d4! cxd4 9.Nxd4 Nxd4 10.Qxd4, you've reached an IQP-style position with the queen centralised, knight on d4, and Black's pieces still scrambled. The bishop on f1 will come out later to a more active square once the position settles. Classic Rossolimo squeeze.

variations.name: Rossolimo 3.Bb5 g6
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 g6 O-O Bg7 Re1 e5 Bxc6 dxc6 d3
variations.explanation: Against the fianchetto setup, the Rossolimo gets concrete. Black plays 3...g6 + 4...Bg7 preparing to put the bishop on the long diagonal. You respond 5.Re1 e5 (Black tries to lock the centre supporting the fianchetto) 6.Bxc6! dxc6 (Black recaptures with the d-pawn to keep the central pawn structure) 7.d3 — locking in a long-term structural advantage. Black has doubled c-pawns and the half-open d-file is yours. The bishop on g7 looks active but it's biting on YOUR central pawn on d3 — defended. Position favours the better long-term technique.

variations.name: Accelerated Pawn Storm (Carlsen-inspired)
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 e6 Bxc6 bxc6 d3 d5 e5
variations.explanation: Magnus Carlsen popularised this approach — trade on c6 IMMEDIATELY after Black's …e6 setup. After 4.Bxc6 bxc6 (Black takes with the b-pawn keeping the c-file under control) 5.d3 d5 6.e5! — you push the e-pawn to cramp Black, the c-pawn structure (now doubled and weak) is a permanent target. White's plan: develop with Nbd2, h3, O-O, then attack on the kingside while Black struggles to find counterplay with the broken queenside. Carlsen has won multiple games in this line at the highest level.

openings.name: Ruy Lopez (Carlsen)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 d6 c3 O-O h3 Nb8 d4 Nbd7
openings.overview: Magnus plays the Ruy Lopez the way it was meant to be played — as a 60-move chess clinic. Named after Spanish bishop Ruy López de Segura who analyzed it in his 1561 book *Libro del Ajedrez*, the Spanish Game has been the king of 1.e4 openings for 460 years. Steinitz refined the theory in the 1880s; Tarrasch made it the foundation of his 'rules of position'; Capablanca, Lasker, Alekhine, and every world champion since has spent years in its mainlines. Fischer reportedly said 'best by test' about 1.e4 specifically because the Ruy Lopez exists. Magnus's Ruy Lopez record at the highest level is the most dominant in modern history: he's won repeatedly from positions where Stockfish gives White +0.15. The opening teaches you something most chess teachers won't: you don't need an advantage to win. You need to make the second-best move on every turn, while your opponent makes the third-best.
keyIdeas: Bb5 → Ba4 → Bb3 is one bishop's journey. The first move pins, the second retreats from the …a6 chase, the third repositions where it stays for the whole game (eyeing f7 from the a2-g8 diagonal). Don't lose patience with the bishop tour — it's not wasted time.

The h3 move is the most important non-developing move in the opening. It prevents the …Bg4 pin AND prepares the future Nh2-g4 manoeuvre AND creates a luft for the king. Looks like a tempo loss; actually it's an investment.

Build c3 + d4 SLOWLY. The d4 push only happens after castling, h3, AND Re1 — you're not racing. The patience IS the strategy: you reach a slightly better position with everything coordinated while Black reaches an equal position with awkward pieces.

Carlsen's signature move is to AVOID exchanges that simplify. In Ruy positions where most GMs trade pieces and play for draw, Magnus keeps queens on, keeps minor pieces, and waits for the opponent to make one inaccuracy. That patience is the whole opening's reward.
traps: If Black plays the natural 5...Nxe4 against the Ruy (the Open Ruy), watch for the 6.d4! immediate centre challenge — after 6...b5 7.Bb3 d5 the position is sharp but White's pawn on e5 (after exchanges) is a strong cramping piece.
warnings: DON'T play d4 before h3 + Re1 + c3. Premature d4 lets Black play …exd4 followed by the …Bg4 pin without your h3 prophylaxis. Magnus has a specific move order: c3 first (move 6), h3 next (move 7), THEN Re1 (move 8), THEN d4 (move 9+).

If Black plays the Marshall Gambit (8...d5!? instead of …d6), Magnus has a specific anti-Marshall with 8.a4 — don't accept the gambit, prevent it. The Marshall is too sharp for the slow technical style.

variations.name: Closed Ruy Lopez Main Line
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 d6 c3 O-O h3 Nb8 d4 Nbd7
variations.explanation: The Breyer Variation — Magnus's preferred Ruy Lopez setup. Watch the slow strategic dance. The bishop tour Bb5 → Ba4 → Bb3 takes three moves but positions the bishop perfectly. Black's mysterious 9...Nb8 is the Breyer's signature — retreating the knight to reposition via …Nbd7 (defending e5 indirectly and freeing the c-pawn for …c5). White meanwhile sets up the full classical Ruy structure: c3 + h3 + Re1 + d4. Both sides have completed development; the middlegame is one of the deepest in chess. Magnus's record from this position is the basis of multiple World Championship matches.

variations.name: Anti-Marshall with 8.a4
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O a4 b4 d3 d6 Nbd2 Na5 Ba2 c5
variations.explanation: Instead of allowing the Marshall Gambit (8...d5!? with crazy attacking chess), Magnus plays 8.a4! probing the queenside before Black commits. After 8...b4 (closing the queenside) 9.d3 (NOT d4 — keep the structure flexible), the position becomes positional manoeuvring chess. Black's 9...d6 + 10.Nbd2 Na5 (kicking the bishop to a2) 11.Ba2 c5 — both sides set up for a long slow squeeze. The a4 system avoided the Marshall completely; Magnus is now playing a position with a slight space edge and no theoretical risks. Pure championship-style chess.

variations.name: Berlin Wall Endgame
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 Nd6 Bxc6 dxc6 dxe5 Nf5 Qxd8+ Kxd8 Nc3 Ke8 h3 Be7 Bf4 Be6
variations.explanation: The Berlin Wall — the most famous endgame in modern chess (Kramnik used it to dethrone Kasparov in 2000). When Black plays 3...Nf6 instead of the main 3...a6, White can force the queen trade with 4.O-O Nxe4 5.d4 Nd6 6.Bxc6 dxc6 7.dxe5 Nf5 8.Qxd8+ Kxd8. The resulting position is queenless — Black has the bishop pair AND the doubled c-pawns (a trade-off). The middlegame plays itself technically: White has a slight edge from better pawn structure, Black has bishop pair compensation. Carlsen has converted this position dozens of times against the world's best — the technique is the opening's entire point.

openings.name: Catalan Opening (Carlsen)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 a4 Bd7 Qxc4 Bc6 Bf4 Nbd7
openings.overview: If the Ruy Lopez is Magnus's 1.e4 weapon, the Catalan is his 1.d4 weapon — and it's even more dominant. The opening was christened at the 1929 Barcelona tournament when GM Savielly Tartakower, asked for a new variation by the organizers, fianchettoed his king's bishop and called it 'the Catalan' in honour of the host region. Botvinnik and Kasparov used it sparingly; it became a true champion's weapon when Vladimir Kramnik adopted it in the 2000s, and Magnus has converted it into his signature 1.d4 weapon — winning World Championship games from positions Stockfish calls equal because the Bg2 quietly outlasts Black's c8-bishop across 50 moves. The Catalan teaches you what 'small advantages' really means — they're not 'almost equal,' they're 'eventually decisive.'
keyIdeas: The Bg2 is the entire opening. From g2 it aims at a8, b7, c6, d5, e4, f3 — the longest diagonal in chess. NEVER trade this bishop unless you're winning material; the Catalan without the g2-bishop is just a worse Queen's Gambit.

When Black plays …dxc4 (the Open Catalan), don't recapture immediately — first play Qc2 + a4 setting up the recovery. The pawn isn't going anywhere because Bg2 prevents Black from holding it with …b5. After Qxc4 you've got back the pawn AND your queen is centralised.

a4 is the Catalan's most important pawn move. It prevents Black's …b5 (which would defend c4 and develop the c8-bishop to b7), keeps the queenside cramped, and creates the long-term weakness on the b6-square that the Bg2 can eventually exploit.

Don't push e4 early. Most amateurs reach for e4 too soon (it 'feels right'); but in the Catalan, the e4 push gives Black equalising counterplay. The right plan is slow piece improvement — Bf4 to a more active square, Nbd2 → Nb3 → Nd2 reroutes, Rfc1, Qb3 piling up.
traps: If Black tries to keep the c4-pawn with the natural-looking …b5, you play a4! undermining. After …c6 (defending b5), 9.axb5 cxb5 — Black's queenside pawns are wrecked AND the c-file is open for your rooks. Greedy c4-pawn-holding is one of the Catalan's biggest punishments.
warnings: DON'T play e4 in the first 15 moves. The Catalan is patience chess — Black has fewer good ideas the longer the centre stays closed. Premature e4 dissolves your structural advantage.

Respect Black's …c5 break. In some lines (after Black plays …Nbd7 + …b6 + …Bb7), the …c5 break opens the centre on Black's terms — and your Bg2's diagonal can become irrelevant if the centre opens with pawns in the wrong place.

variations.name: Open Catalan Main Line
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 a4 Bd7 Qxc4 Bc6 Bf4 Nbd7
variations.explanation: Magnus's flagship Catalan line. After 6...dxc4 (the Open Catalan — Black grabs the pawn), you DON'T recapture immediately. 7.Qc2 prepares the recovery WHILE pinning Black's queenside. 7...a6 (Black tries to hold c4 with …b5) 8.a4! — the killer prophylaxis. Now …b5 hangs to axb5. Black plays 8...Bd7 (no other useful developing square) 9.Qxc4 — pawn recovered. 9...Bc6 (Black trades the bishop on the long diagonal to neutralise your Bg2) 10.Bf4 Nbd7 — both sides develop. The bishop trade on c6 is forced eventually; the question is on whose terms. Magnus's positions from here win endgames through superior technique.

variations.name: Closed Catalan
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O Nbd7 Qc2 c6 Nbd2 b6 e4 Bb7 e5 Ne8
variations.explanation: When Black plays the Closed Catalan with 6...Nbd7 (keeping d5 supported rather than taking on c4), you reach a calmer position. 7.Qc2 c6 8.Nbd2 b6 (Black prepares …Bb7 fianchetto) 9.e4! — NOW you play e4, because Black has committed to a closed setup where the e4 push gains space without dissolving your structural edge. 9...Bb7 10.e5 Ne8 — Black's knight is shoved to e8 (passive), the centre stays closed, and you have a long-term space advantage. The plan from here: Nb3 (rerouting to support queenside), Re1, Bd3 — slow improvement of every piece.

openings.name: English Opening (Carlsen)
openings.pgn: c4 e5 Nc3 Nf6 Nf3 Nc6 g3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 Bg2 Nb6 O-O Be7 d3 O-O Be3 Be6 Rc1 f6
openings.overview: Most chess teachers tell you to pick ONE opening and learn it deeply. Magnus picked the English partly because he can play it as five DIFFERENT openings depending on what Black does. Named for Howard Staunton (a British player) who used 1.c4 against Saint-Amant in their 1843 match — the first international chess contest ever — the English Opening became a mainstream weapon when Mikhail Botvinnik used it in his 1948 World Championship victory, and Bobby Fischer played it in critical games of his 1972 Spassky match (notable departures from his usual 1.e4). The English is the world champion's anti-preparation weapon: by move 4 you've sidestepped 95% of opening theory and your opponent is improvising. Magnus uses it specifically against opponents who've prepared 1.e4 lines for him — the surprise factor at the highest level is worth half a point.
keyIdeas: The d5-square is the strategic battleground. Every English variation revolves around control of d5 — the c4-pawn supports it, the g2-bishop attacks it, the Nc3-knight can land on it. If you control d5, you control the position.

Don't commit your central pawn structure early. The English's flexibility is its weapon — by NOT playing d4 in the first 10 moves, you keep the options of d3 (King's Indian Attack style), d4 (transpose to QGD), or even no central pawn push at all (pure pieces and fianchetto play).

Watch for Reverse Sicilian opportunities. If Black plays 1...e5, you're now in a Sicilian a tempo up. Use that tempo to play the closed Sicilian setup (Bg2 + d3 + Be3) and outplay Black in slow strategic chess.
The g3 + Bg2 fianchetto is the English's spine. Don't deviate without a specific reason — the bishop's permanent home is g2 where it eyes the long diagonal AND defends g3 (no king-side weaknesses).
traps: After 1.c4 e5 (Reverse Sicilian), if Black overextends with 4...d5 5.cxd5 Nxd5, the bishop on g2 stares straight at d5 + b7. Combined with Nc3 attacking Nd5, Black's queen often has to come to d8 wasting tempos — pure positional tempo theft.
warnings: DON'T play d4 in the first 6 moves. The English is FLEXIBLE — once d4 is played, you've committed to a Queen's pawn structure and Black's prep helps them. Keep d-pawn options open as long as possible.

Reverse Sicilian structures favour the side that plays patiently. If you try to attack like an Open Sicilian player from the White side, you'll find your own setup doesn't quite work — adapt to slow strategic chess.

variations.name: Reversed Sicilian
variations.pgn: c4 e5 Nc3 Nf6 Nf3 Nc6 g3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 Bg2 Nb6 O-O Be7 d3 O-O Be3 Be6
variations.explanation: When Black plays 1...e5, you're now playing a Sicilian with the white pieces and an extra tempo. After 2.Nc3 Nf6 3.Nf3 Nc6 4.g3 d5 (Black challenges the centre) 5.cxd5 Nxd5 — you've reached a position structurally identical to a Sicilian Dragon, but YOU have the extra move. 6.Bg2 Nb6 (the d5-knight retreats) 7.O-O Be7 8.d3 (modest, not d4 — keep flexibility) O-O 9.Be3 — a classic English setup. White's plan: Qd2, Nd5 manoeuvre, eventual b4 expansion. The extra tempo means you reach attacking positions one move faster than the regular Open Sicilian permits. Magnus's blitz games are full of these positions.

variations.name: Symmetrical English
variations.pgn: c4 c5 Nc3 Nf6 Nf3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e3 Nc6 Bc4 Nb6 Bb3
variations.explanation: Black plays 1...c5 mirroring — a common amateur reply that hopes for symmetry. You break the symmetry on move 5: instead of fianchetto, play 5.e3 followed by 6.Bc4! The bishop comes out aggressively eyeing f7 (the standard target). After 6...Nb6 (Black kicks) 7.Bb3 (retreat) you've reached a Hedgehog-style structure but with White's pieces more active. The Bb3 + Nf3 setup also enables a future Maroczy Bind (with d3 + e4) cramping Black's queenside. Symmetrical structures favour the better-developed side; you've developed faster.

openings.name: Sicilian Sveshnikov (Carlsen)
openings.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Nd5 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6 c3 O-O Nc2 Bg5
openings.overview: Magnus chose the Sveshnikov for his 2018 World Championship match against Caruana — a defining moment in modern opening theory. The opening is named after Soviet GM Evgeny Sveshnikov, who pioneered the …e5 idea in the 1970s when chess orthodoxy said you should never voluntarily create a weakness on d5. He spent years analyzing it, publishing his findings in a 1988 book, and proved that the structural concession was worth the dynamic compensation. The Sveshnikov became Kasparov's auxiliary weapon in the 1990s, and Carlsen's championship-clinching choice in 2018 cemented its elite status. Black's bishop pair plus dynamic piece play overcomes the structural concession every time, IF you know what you're doing. Magnus knew. He drew Caruana every game with Black using this opening — and the line he chose is now copy-pasted into every World-class repertoire.
keyIdeas: …e5 is the move that names the opening. After 5.Nc3, Black plays 5...e5 KICKING White's d4-knight to b5 — a paradoxical move that gives up the d5-square but gains the bishop pair and central pawn structure.

The d5-square IS weak — embrace it. White will park a knight on d5 and you can't prevent it. Your compensation: the bishop pair, the half-open f-file (after the eventual …f5 break), and the dynamic piece play that comes from a more active Black queen and rook structure.

The …f5 break is your strategic goal for the entire game. Once you achieve …f5, the kingside opens, your rooks reach the half-open f-file, and the bishop pair on b7 + g7 (after fianchetto) aims at White's king. Without …f5, the Sveshnikov becomes a slow loss; with …f5, it's a powerful attacking opening.

Magnus's signature: don't fear the structural weakness. Most amateurs hate playing positions where they have a 'hole' on a key square. The Sveshnikov teaches you that holes are only weaknesses if the opponent can EXPLOIT them — and with active pieces, you can prevent the exploitation.
traps: After 5.Nc3 e5 6.Ndb5 d6 7.Bg5 a6 8.Na3 — Black's natural 8...b5 is the principled move. But timing is critical: if you delay …b5 by one move (playing …Be7 first), White's Bxf6 + Nd5 sequence wins time and reaches a structurally better version of the position.
warnings: The d5-square needs constant attention. If you let White park a knight there permanently (Nd5 with no Black piece able to challenge it), the position becomes positionally lost. Plan …Nbd7-Nb6 or …Be7-…Bxf6/g5 routes to challenge the d5-outpost.

Beware the Na3-Nc2-Ne3 manoeuvre. White's a3-knight (after 8.Na3) re-routes via c2 to e3 to f5 (eventually), AND blocks your …f5 break by occupying f5 first. Time your moves to prevent this rerouting.

variations.name: Main Line with 9.Nd5
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Nd5 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6 c3 O-O Nc2 Bg5
variations.explanation: The Sveshnikov's main highway — every elite-level Sicilian player has studied this position. After Black's 8...b5 9.Nd5! White plants the knight on d5, and 9...Be7 (defending f6) 10.Bxf6 (trading the dark-squared bishop) Bxf6 — Black keeps the bishop pair. 11.c3 (preparing Nc2-Ne3 reroute) O-O 12.Nc2 — White prepares the long-term squeeze of the d5-outpost. Black's 12...Bg5! is the signature — the bishop swings back to active play, eyes f4 or e3, and prepares the …f5 break. Carlsen-Caruana 2018 World Championship match featured 5 games in this exact position, all drawn.

variations.name: Chelyabinsk Variation
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Ndb5 d6 Bg5 a6 Na3 b5 Bxf6 gxf6 Nd5 f5 Bd3 Be6 O-O Bg7
variations.explanation: When White plays 9.Bxf6 (instead of 9.Nd5), Black has a choice: recapture with bishop or with g-pawn. The Chelyabinsk recaptures with the g-pawn: 9...gxf6! Yes, doubled f-pawns AND a wrecked kingside structure — but the resulting position has the half-open g-file for Black's rook AND the trumps of the bishop pair on b7+g7 (after fianchetto). 10.Nd5 f5! immediately — Black plays the …f5 break THE NEXT MOVE, accepting the king exposure for the open files. After 11.Bd3 Be6 12.O-O Bg7 the position is wild: White's king is safer but Black has multiple files for attack. This is the most ambitious Sveshnikov choice.

openings.name: Berlin Defense (Carlsen)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 Nd6 Bxc6 dxc6 dxe5 Nf5 Qxd8+ Kxd8 Nc3 Ke8 h3 Be7 Bf4 Be6 Rad1 h6
openings.overview: Vladimir Kramnik used the Berlin Wall to dethrone Garry Kasparov in the 2000 World Championship match in London — Kasparov scored ZERO wins against the Berlin in their 15-game match, the first time in his career he failed to win a championship game with White. The opening itself has older roots — Siegbert Tarrasch documented the …Nf6 defence in his late 1800s analyses — but Kramnik's match made it the elite weapon of choice for the next two decades. Magnus inherited the system and uses it as his trusted '1.e4 emergency draw weapon'. The miracle of the Berlin: by trading queens on move 8, Black REMOVES White's main attacking pieces. What remains is a complex endgame where Black has the bishop pair, slightly doubled pawns, and a king walking to e8 (yes, e8 — not castled). It looks suspicious. It's actually fine. Magnus has won countless games from this position by simply outlasting opponents who think 'the queens are off, must be a draw.'
keyIdeas: The queen trade is FORCED, not optional. After 5.O-O Nxe4 6.d4 Nd6 7.Bxc6 dxc6 8.dxe5 Nf5 9.Qxd8+ Kxd8 — that's the Berlin Wall position. EVERY Black move was best; if you deviate at any of these steps, you're playing a worse opening.

The king on d8 (then e8 then often f7) is FINE. It looks exposed but White's attacking pieces are gone — no queens, soon to trade rooks. The king becomes an active piece in the endgame, walking to centre via Ke8-Kf7 like a typical endgame king.

Both bishops on c8 and f8 are your trumps. Don't trade them lightly. The bishop pair in an open queenless endgame is worth roughly 0.5 pawns — exactly the structural compensation you need for the doubled c-pawns.

The c5 break is the endgame's strategic theme. After …c5 (sometimes prepared by …Bd7, …Ke8-f7, …Re8), Black opens the centre, activates the bishops, and the doubled c-pawns become irrelevant when one of them queens.
traps: After 9.Qxd8+ Kxd8 10.Nc3 — if White plays the casual 10...Ke8?? immediately, Black walks into 11.Nxe5! Be6 12.Nxc6+ winning a pawn for nothing. The correct move order is to play …Be6 or …Bd7 first BEFORE walking the king.
warnings: DON'T castle in the Berlin. The king's journey is e8 → f7 (walked over multiple moves) — NEVER short-castle which puts the king on g8 where it's actually MORE exposed in the queenless middlegame because White's pieces aim at the kingside.

Be patient. The Berlin's advantage is positional and structural — bishop pair, eventual …c5 break, slightly better pawn structure. There are no tactical fireworks. If you try to 'win quickly' you'll lose the structural edge by overextending.

variations.name: Berlin Endgame Main Line
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 Nd6 Bxc6 dxc6 dxe5 Nf5 Qxd8+ Kxd8 Nc3 Ke8 h3 Be7 Bf4 Be6 Rad1 h6
variations.explanation: The Berlin Wall position in its purest form. After 5.O-O Nxe4 6.d4 Nd6 7.Bxc6 dxc6 8.dxe5 Nf5 — Black's knight reroutes to f5 (the only good square) and 9.Qxd8+ Kxd8! — the king takes voluntarily. 10.Nc3 Ke8 (the king starts walking back to safety) 11.h3 (prophylaxis) Be7 12.Bf4 (developing the bishop to a more active diagonal) Be6 13.Rad1 (centralising the rook) h6 — Black's typical defensive move. Position assessment: White has a slight structural edge (e5-pawn intact, no doubled pawns); Black has the bishop pair AND will eventually push …c5 freeing everything. Engines call this position +0.20 for White; Magnus's win rate from Black is around 50%.

variations.name: Berlin with 4.d3 (Anti-Berlin)
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 d3 Bc5 Bxc6 dxc6 Nbd2 Be6 O-O Bd6 Nb3 Qe7
variations.explanation: When White avoids the Berlin Wall entirely with 4.d3 (the Anti-Berlin), Black plays a different game: 4...Bc5 develops aggressively, 5.Bxc6 dxc6 (Black takes the doubled-pawn structure voluntarily — the bishop pair is worth it), 6.Nbd2 Be6 7.O-O Bd6 (rerouting the bishop). The …Bd6 + …Qe7 + …O-O-O setup signals a long-side castle ambition. Black gets the bishop pair without entering the queenless endgame — a more dynamic version of the standard Berlin treatment.

openings.name: Queen's Gambit Declined (Carlsen)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Be7 Bf4 O-O e3 Nbd7 c5 Nh5 Bd3 Nxf4 exf4 b6 b4 a5 a3 Ba6
openings.overview: Magnus's QGD is a love letter to classical chess. The defence is documented in Spanish manuscripts dating to the 1490s, became serious tournament theory in the 1880s when Wilhelm Steinitz adopted it, was perfected by Siegbert Tarrasch (the 'Praeceptor Germaniae' — Germany's chess teacher) at the turn of the century, and reached its peak with Capablanca who made it his lifelong defence. Karpov played it through his entire 1975-1985 World Championship reign without losing a single QGD game with Black. Magnus uses it specifically when he needs reliability — against in-form opponents, in must-not-lose games, in matches where one mistake costs everything. The QGD doesn't 'win' the opening; it equalises by move 15 and then asks your opponent to outplay you for the next 40. They usually can't. This isn't Magnus's flashy opening — it's his foundation.
keyIdeas: d5 + e6 + Nf6 + Be7 + O-O — that's the QGD setup, in that order, every game. Master this five-piece formation before you worry about anything else. The structure won't lose; only the player can lose.

The c8-bishop is the QGD's only real problem. Three solutions exist: (a) Tartakower's …b6 + …Bb7 fianchetto, (b) classical …Nbd7 + …c6 + …Bd7-Be8-f5, or (c) trade off via …Bb4 (Ragozin variation). Always know which solution you're playing for BEFORE move 8.

…c5 is the equalising break. The right moment: when you've castled, completed minor piece development, and White hasn't yet played c5 themselves. After …c5 the centre opens, your pieces breathe, and the structure becomes balanced.
The QGD's secret: it's harder for White to PLAY than for Black. White has to find a plan; Black just defends and waits. Magnus's superpower is patience — outwaiting opponents who run out of ideas.
traps: If White plays cxd5 too early (the Exchange Variation) before completing development, the resulting IQP-shaped position favours Black — your pieces are already harmoniously placed, White's aren't. Welcome the cxd5 exchange when White hasn't yet played Nf3.
warnings: NEVER take on c4 early. The …dxc4 capture surrenders the central tension AND gives White a tempo when Bxc4 develops with attack. Save …dxc4 for specific Meran-like moments, not as a default response.

Watch for kingside attacks when the centre closes. In the QGD lines where White locks the centre with c5, White's plan switches to a kingside attack with f4-f5 — be ready to defend with …Re8 + …Nf8 + …g6 prophylaxis.

variations.name: 5.Bf4 System
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Be7 Bf4 O-O e3 Nbd7 c5 Nh5 Bd3 Nxf4 exf4 b6 b4 a5 a3 Ba6
variations.explanation: Magnus's preferred QGD line against the active 5.Bf4. After 5...Be7 6.e3 O-O 7.c5! (White grabs space by closing the queenside) 7...Nh5! (the signature counter — Black attacks the bishop on f4 immediately, refusing to let White consolidate). 8.Bd3 Nxf4 9.exf4 — Black accepts White's doubled f-pawns in exchange for the bishop trade. Now the structural battle begins: White has space (c5 + d4 + f4); Black has the bishop pair AND queenside undermining ideas with 9...b6 (challenging c5) 10.b4 (White defends with another pawn) 10...a5 11.a3 (White holds) 11...Ba6 — Black trades the light-squared bishop too, simplifying into a balanced middlegame. Pure strategic chess.

variations.name: Tartakower Variation
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Be7 Bg5 h6 Bh4 O-O e3 b6 cxd5 Nxd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 Nxd5 exd5 Rc1 Be6 Bd3 c5
variations.explanation: The Tartakower solves the c8-bishop problem with a fianchetto. After 5.Bg5 h6 6.Bh4 O-O 7.e3 b6! — Black announces the plan: …Bb7 is coming. White's typical reply 8.cxd5 Nxd5 9.Bxe7 Qxe7 10.Nxd5 exd5 — massive simplification. Now Black plays 11...Be6 (developing the c8-bishop on e6 instead of b7, after the centre opened) 12.Bd3 c5! — the equalising break with full development complete. The Tartakower's elegance: it solves the bishop problem AND reaches a balanced middlegame in a forced sequence of trades.

variations.name: Ragozin Variation
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Bb4 Bg5 dxc4 e4 c5 Bxc4 cxd4 Nxd4 Qa5 Bd2 Qc5
variations.explanation: The Ragozin is the most aggressive QGD answer — 4...Bb4 pins the c3-knight immediately, before White can consolidate. After 5.Bg5 dxc4 6.e4 (White grabs the centre) c5 7.Bxc4 cxd4 (chain of central trades) 8.Nxd4 Qa5 — Black's queen leaps to the queenside threatening multiple ideas (Qxg5, pinning the d4-knight if it moves). 9.Bd2 Qc5 — the queen probes both wings. Magnus uses this when he wants dynamic chances; the imbalanced position (Black has gambited central control for piece activity) creates winning chances for both sides.

openings.name: Queen's Pawn London (Carlsen)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 Nf3 d5 Bf4 c5 e3 Nc6 Nbd2 e6 c3 Bd6 Bg3 O-O Bd3 b6 O-O Bb7 Ne5
openings.overview: Magnus took the London System — long considered a beginner's weapon — and made it a World Championship-level opening. The system has been around since the 1922 London tournament where it earned its name, but it remained club-level chess for nearly a century. Carlsen's 2010s adoption of the London at elite level (including critical games in his World Championship matches) shocked the chess world and demonstrated that systems-based openings could compete with the deepest theoretical lines. His insight: the London isn't passive; it's PATIENT. Play the same pieces on the same squares every game (Bf4, e3, c3, Nbd2, Bd3, O-O), reach a flexible middlegame, and start outplaying. Carlsen's London games against top opposition have a 60%+ win rate as White — because the LACK of theoretical risk lets him focus 100% on the middlegame battle his opponent has to navigate.
keyIdeas: Bf4 BEFORE e3 — non-negotiable. If you play e3 first, the dark-squared bishop is locked behind its own pawn permanently. This is the single most important move-order rule in the London.

The Ne5 outpost is Magnus's signature. After full development, the Nf3-knight jumps to e5 — a square no Black piece can permanently dislodge it from. Combined with Bf4 + Bd3 pointing at the kingside, the Ne5 becomes the heart of any attack.

Build slowly: d4 + e3 + c3 + Nbd2 + Bd3 + O-O is the standard setup, regardless of what Black does. Same pieces, same squares, every game. Then make ONE move that fits the specific position (Ne5, h3, a4, or Re1).

The e4 break is reserved for the late middlegame. Most amateurs play e4 too soon (wanting action); Magnus waits 25 moves until the structure is ripe, then e4 opens lines for ALL the developed pieces simultaneously.
traps: If Black tries the standard …Nh5 (attacking Bf4 hoping for a trade), the bishop retreats to g3 where it stays USEFUL — controls h4, defends h2, and the h5-knight becomes a target for a future Ne5 or h3+g4.
warnings: DON'T play e3 before Bf4. Doing so blocks the c1-bishop forever and you've reached a worse Colle System instead of a London.
The b2-pawn is the London's only structural weakness. Black's …c5 + …Qb6 hits it; always have c3 ready (which you should be playing anyway) — never leave b2 undefended without a piece backing it up.

variations.name: Standard London vs d5
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Nf3 d5 Bf4 c5 e3 Nc6 Nbd2 e6 c3 Bd6 Bg3 O-O Bd3 b6 O-O Bb7 Ne5
variations.explanation: Carlsen's standard treatment. Both sides develop in textbook order: 5...c5 (Black challenges centre) 6.e3 (NOT before Bf4!) Nc6 7.Nbd2 e6 8.c3 (defending b2 prophylactically) 9.Bg3 (the bishop retreats from a potential …Bd6 trade, keeping its useful diagonal) 10.Bd3 b6 11.O-O Bb7 12.Ne5! — the signature move. The knight reaches its dream outpost on e5, supported by f4 (after Bf4 swung to g3, the f-pawn is free to move). Black has full development but White has the more active piece structure. The middlegame plan: f4 push, Rfb3 lift, Qf3 swing — slowly building a kingside attack while the centre stays closed.

variations.name: London vs King's Indian
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Nf3 g6 Bf4 Bg7 e3 O-O Be2 d6 O-O Nbd7 h3 c5 c3 b6 Nbd2 Bb7 a4 a6
variations.explanation: When Black fianchettos with 2...g6 + 3...Bg7, the London adapts. Magnus uses Be2 instead of Bd3 (because the …Bd3-h7 attacking idea doesn't work against a fianchettoed bishop on g7 covering h7). After 4.e3 O-O 5.Be2 d6 6.O-O Nbd7 7.h3 (prophylaxis against …Ng4 ideas) c5 8.c3 b6 9.Nbd2 Bb7 10.a4 a6 — both sides develop calmly. The structure favours White's space; Black's typical KID kingside attack doesn't work because there's no exposed king to attack.

variations.name: Jobava-London Attack
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Nc3 d5 Bf4 c5 e3 cxd4 exd4 a6 Nf3 Nc6 Bd3 Bg4
variations.explanation: The more aggressive Jobava version — Nc3 before Nf3, which threatens Nb5 hitting c7. Black plays the principled 3...d5 (otherwise Nb5 forks come), 4.Bf4 c5 (challenging centre) 5.e3 cxd4 (Black opens the position) 6.exd4 a6 (preventing Nb5 forever) 7.Nf3 Nc6 8.Bd3 Bg4 — Black tries the …Bg4 pin. White's reply: h3 + Bh5 forcing the bishop to commit, then standard London development. The Jobava version is sharper than the pure London because the Nc3 can re-route to b5/d2/e2 dynamically based on what Black does.

openings.name: Sicilian Najdorf (Carlsen)
openings.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be2 e5 Nb3 Be7 O-O O-O Be3 Be6 Qd2 Nbd7 a4 Qc7
openings.overview: Magnus's Najdorf is the positional Najdorf — quiet pieces, solid king, slowly accumulated advantages. He doesn't play the Bg5 Poisoned Pawn lines that Fischer made famous in his 1972 World Championship match (Fischer's Game 11 vs Spassky in the Poisoned Pawn is among the most analysed in chess history); he plays the calm 6.Be2 setup that Karpov pioneered in the 1970s where Black gets a stable position and waits for White to overpress. Magnus has also developed the modern 6.Be3 …a5 prophylactic system that Naroditsky also champions. The Najdorf in Magnus's hands has the highest practical drawing rate of any Sicilian — and when he wants the full point, he simply outplays opponents in the endgame. This is the Najdorf as a marathon weapon, not a sprint weapon.
keyIdeas: …Be7 + …O-O quickly is Magnus's signature against ALL Najdorf systems — including the English Attack. While other players castle long or delay castling, Magnus prioritises king safety and accepts the position is going to be a long technical battle.

…e5 closes the centre and creates the typical Najdorf hole on d5. Don't worry about the hole — it's a manageable weakness if you keep …Nbd7 and …Nb6 ready to challenge any White knight that lands there.

Queenside expansion with …Qc7 + …Rc8 + …b5 is the Najdorf's strategic theme. The timing of …b5 matters: only after the queenside is fully prepared, never before White has played a4 (which makes axb5 a real threat).

Magnus's endgame edge: the Najdorf endgames after the queens trade favour the side with better technique. With a slight space disadvantage, Magnus reaches drawn endgames against grandmasters; against everyone else, he wins them.
traps: Be careful with …b5 timing. After White plays a4 (preventing …b5), the natural-looking …b5? hangs to axb5 opening the a-file for White's rook. The right order: prepare with …Nbd7 + …Rb8 first, THEN consider …b5.
warnings: Avoid the Bg5 Poisoned Pawn lines (with …Qxb2). Magnus deliberately doesn't play these — they're too sharp and theoretical. If you want the Najdorf for stability, accept the slight worse-but-defensible positions in the Bg5 e6 system instead.

Watch for the Nd5 sacrifice. In all Najdorf systems where White has Be3 + Qd2 + O-O-O, the thematic Nd5! sacrifice (Nxd5 exd5 + e5 push) breaks Black's centre open. Defend with …e6 or prepare …Nxd5 to avoid the e5 cramp.

variations.name: 6.Be2 Positional
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be2 e5 Nb3 Be7 O-O O-O Be3 Be6 Qd2 Nbd7 a4 Qc7
variations.explanation: Magnus's flagship Najdorf line. After 6.Be2 (the most restrained White system), Black plays the textbook 6...e5! 7.Nb3 (the knight is shoved back) Be7 (calm development, not the sharp Bg5 lines) 8.O-O O-O 9.Be3 Be6 (controls the d5 outpost) 10.Qd2 Nbd7 (the knight reroutes to support the queenside) 11.a4 (White prevents …b5) Qc7 — the queen reaches its standard square. Position assessment: White has a slight space edge, Black has the solid setup. The middlegame plan: …Rfc8 + eventual …b6 + …Bb7 fianchetto, then slow strategic chess. Magnus's win rate from this position at elite level is well above 50% as Black.

variations.name: 6.Bg5 e6 Classical
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Bg5 e6 f4 Be7 Qf3 Qc7 O-O-O Nbd7 Bd3 b5 Rhe1 Bb7
variations.explanation: Against the sharp 6.Bg5, Magnus chooses the QUIET classical setup. 6...e6 (not the Poisoned Pawn 6...Qb6!?) 7.f4 Be7 8.Qf3 Qc7 9.O-O-O Nbd7 — both sides castle on opposite wings, the race is on. But Magnus's version is calmer: 10.Bd3 b5 11.Rhe1 Bb7 — solid Black development without sharp tactics. The position becomes a long-term opposite-side-castling race where structural advantages matter more than tempo races.

variations.name: Anti-English Attack
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be3 e5 Nb3 Be6 f3 h5 Qd2 Nbd7 O-O-O Be7 Kb1 Qc7
variations.explanation: Against the English Attack (Be3 + Qd2 + O-O-O + g4 storm), Magnus pioneered the prophylactic 8...h5! — preventing White's g4 attack BEFORE it happens. After 6.Be3 e5 7.Nb3 Be6 8.f3 h5! 9.Qd2 Nbd7 10.O-O-O Be7 11.Kb1 Qc7 — Black has slowed White's kingside attack dramatically. The …h5 move is unusual but practical: it weakens g5 slightly but completely neutralises White's main attacking lever. This idea has been adopted by virtually every top Najdorf player since Magnus popularised it.

openings.name: King's Indian Attack (Hikaru)
openings.pgn: Nf3 d5 g3 Nf6 Bg2 e6 O-O Be7 d3 O-O Nbd2 c5 e4 Nc6 Re1 b5 e5 Nd7 Nf1 a5 Bf4 Ba6
openings.overview: Hikaru plays the King's Indian Attack as his blitz-chess Swiss Army knife — one setup, every game, against literally anything Black plays. The KIA is essentially the King's Indian Defence played in reverse — pioneered by Russian master Réti as part of the hypermodern movement in the 1920s, then resurrected by Bobby Fischer who scored brilliant attacking wins with it during the 1960s (his game vs Myagmarsuren at the 1967 Sousse Interzonal is still studied for its violent kingside finish). The opening fits Hikaru's bullet-chess style perfectly because the setup is automatic — same pieces on the same squares — letting him focus 100% on the middlegame. While opponents are remembering theory for ten different White first moves, he's analysing the middlegame from move one. In bullet chess this matters enormously — and Hikaru is the fastest in the world.
keyIdeas: The 'setup before plan' principle: place every piece on its KIA square (Nf3, Bg2, O-O, d3, Nbd2) BEFORE thinking about what to do with them. The plan emerges from the position; the setup is fixed.
e4 followed by e4-e5 is the strategic engine. Once your Nbd2 supports e4, push it. After e5 cramps Black's f6-knight, you have time to reroute pieces to the kingside for the attack.

The kingside attack uses pieces, not pawns initially. The Nf1-h2-g4 reroute (knight from d2 to f1 to h2 to g4) is the KIA's signature manoeuvre — knight reaches a key attacking square WITHOUT compromising king safety.
Against …c5+…e6 (French-like structures), the KIA is at its best. The e5-pawn cramps Black's c8-bishop AND blocks the …f6 break; your knight on g4 + bishop on g2 + rook on e1 all aim at the kingside.
traps: If Black panics and plays …d4 closing the centre, you've won the opening — the d4-pawn isn't attacking anything, but it permanently locks the centre and you can launch the kingside attack with no central counterplay possible. Hikaru's KIA wins are full of opponents who played …d4 without thinking.
warnings: DON'T push e4-e5 if Black can immediately undermine with …f6. The right moment for e5 is after Black has committed to a kingside setup (…Be7, …O-O) where …f6 would weaken their own king.

The KIA looks slow because it IS slow — don't let Black get TOO much queenside space. If Black plays …b5 + …a5 + …Bb7 quickly, you might need to play a4 to prevent the queenside expansion before launching your kingside attack.

variations.name: vs French-like Setup
variations.pgn: Nf3 d5 g3 Nf6 Bg2 e6 O-O Be7 d3 O-O Nbd2 c5 e4 Nc6 Re1 b5 e5 Nd7 Nf1 a5
variations.explanation: The KIA's most common position. Black plays a French-like setup with …d5 + …e6 + …c5. You go through the textbook setup: 2.g3 Nf6 3.Bg2 e6 4.O-O Be7 5.d3 O-O 6.Nbd2 c5 7.e4 — there's your trademark central push. After 7...Nc6 8.Re1 b5 9.e5! (timing it perfectly — Black committed pieces) Nd7 10.Nf1 a5 — White's knight starts the f1-h2-g4 reroute while Black expands queenside. The resulting position is a perfect KIA: kingside attack coming with Nh2-g4 + h4-h5, Black has queenside space but no targets. The position has been Hikaru's bullet bread-and-butter for years.

variations.name: vs Sicilian-like Setup
variations.pgn: Nf3 d5 g3 c5 Bg2 Nc6 O-O e5 d3 Nf6 Nbd2 Be7 e4 O-O Re1 d4 a4 Be6 Nc4 Nd7
variations.explanation: When Black plays the …d5 + …c5 + …e5 setup (a Reverse Sicilian), the KIA adapts. After 6.e4 O-O 7.Re1 — White waits. Black often plays 7...d4 (closing the centre), which White WELCOMES: 8.a4 (preventing …b5) Be6 9.Nc4 — the knight reaches a beautiful outpost on c4, attacking e5 and supporting future a4-a5 ideas. Black has the closed centre they wanted, but White's pieces are perfectly placed for the slow positional grind.

variations.name: vs Caro-Kann Setup
variations.pgn: Nf3 d5 g3 c6 Bg2 Bg4 O-O Nd7 d3 Ngf6 Nbd2 e5 e4 dxe4 dxe4 Bc5 h3 Bh5 Qe1 O-O Nh4 Re8
variations.explanation: Against the Caro-Kann structure (…d5 + …c6 + …Bg4), the KIA is slightly different. Black's …Bg4 pin needs to be addressed. You complete development with 7.d3 Ngf6 8.Nbd2 e5 (Black grabs space) 9.e4! — opening the centre on YOUR terms. After 9...dxe4 10.dxe4 Bc5 11.h3 Bh5 12.Qe1 O-O 13.Nh4 — the knight reroutes to f5 (eventually) and the kingside attack begins. Hikaru's win-rate in this specific setup is among the highest of any elite-level KIA player.

openings.name: Scotch Game (Hikaru)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nxc6 bxc6 e5 Qe7 Qe2 Nd5 c4 Ba6 b3 g6 f4 Bg7
openings.overview: When Hikaru rolls out the Scotch in bullet, he's not surprising opponents — he's announcing 'I'm not playing slow chess today.' The opening's lineage runs through the 1824 London-Edinburgh correspondence match (the Scottish team's opening choice, hence the name), Steinitz's analyses in the 1880s, Kasparov's revival in the 1990s (he played it in his 1990 match against Karpov, breaking the Spanish/Italian monopoly at the top), and now it's a fixture of online speed-chess from Naroditsky's instructional content to Hikaru's bullet streams. Where Naroditsky teaches the Scotch as tempo arithmetic for the long haul, Hikaru uses it as a direct-attack opening: blast open the centre with 3.d4, trade pieces fast with 5.Nxc6, push 6.e5 cramping, and have all pieces on attacking squares by move 12. In bullet this is unbeatable because Black is still trying to remember whether to play …Bc5 or …Nf6 while Hikaru is already attacking.
keyIdeas: 5.Nxc6 is the Hikaru move — not 5.Be3 (Naroditsky's preference). After 5.Nxc6 bxc6 you immediately damage Black's queenside structure AND open the b-file for your rook. The price: you trade off your centralised d4-knight.

Doubled c-pawns are a permanent target. After Nxc6 bxc6, Black has c6 + c7 doubled — a structural weakness that doesn't recover until the endgame. Target the c-pawns with Rc1 + Na4 + Bb3 (eyeing c6 indirectly).

The e5 push is non-optional after Nxc6 bxc6. Play it immediately — it kicks Black's f6-knight, cramps the king-side, AND opens the e-file with no obstruction. Pause for any other move and Black plays …d5 equalising.
Aim for the f4-f5 kingside attack with a4-Ba3 supporting. The Ba3 + f4-f5 + e5-pawn combination creates a vise that's nearly impossible to defend in fast time controls.
traps: After 5.Nxc6 bxc6 6.e5 — Black's queen MUST go to e7 to defend the f6-knight. If Black plays anything else (like 6...Nd5? thinking it 'centralises'), 7.c4! kicks the knight to a poor square AND attacks while keeping the e5-pawn cramping.
warnings: Don't let Black consolidate with …d5 + …Bf5. If you give Black two moves of breathing room, the doubled c-pawns become defensible and the position equalises. The Scotch's edge is the IMMEDIATE pressure, not the long-term endgame.
Watch the c4-pawn carefully. Once you play 7.c4 attacking the d5-knight, Black has resources like …Ba6 (attacking c4) — be ready with b3 supporting OR allow the trade if it serves you.

variations.name: Scotch 4...Nf6 5.Nxc6
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nxc6 bxc6 e5 Qe7 Qe2 Nd5 c4 Ba6 b3 g6 f4 Bg7
variations.explanation: Hikaru's full Scotch attacking sequence. After 5.Nxc6 bxc6 6.e5 (the forcing cramp) Qe7 (only square — defending Nf6 from the e5-pawn capture) 7.Qe2 (pinning the f6-knight) Nd5 (the knight goes where it has to) 8.c4 (kicking) Ba6 (Black hits the c4-pawn) 9.b3 (supporting) g6 10.f4 (kingside attack starts) Bg7 — Black has reached full development but White has a beautiful attacking position. The pawn chain e5+f4 cramps Black's kingside; the Ba3 (planned next) will eye the long diagonal; the open b-file is yours for the rook. By move 15, you're typically launching f5 cracking the position open.

variations.name: Scotch 4...Bc5 Classical
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Bc5 Nxc6 Qf6 Qd2 dxc6 Nc3 Ne7
variations.explanation: Against the classical 4...Bc5, Hikaru plays the unusual 5.Nxc6 Qf6 (the queen attacks the knight on c6 AND keeps options) 6.Qd2 (defending the c6-knight indirectly — if Qxc6+ Qxc6, you've kept a defensible position) dxc6 7.Nc3 Ne7 — Black has equalised material but White has the better-coordinated pieces and the easier game. Less ambitious than the 4...Nf6 line but more solid; useful when you want a controllable game without sharp tactics.

openings.name: London System (Hikaru)
openings.pgn: d4 d5 Nf3 Nf6 Bf4 c5 e3 Nc6 c3 Qb6 Qb3 c4 Qc2 Bf5 Qc1 e6 Nbd2 Be7 Be2 O-O O-O
openings.overview: Hikaru's London System is built for bullet — three-second-per-move chess where you don't have time to think about openings. Same pieces every game: Bf4 + e3 + c3 + Nbd2 + Bd3 + O-O. Memorise the move order, play it 10,000 times, and you can reach move 15 in 4 seconds of clock time. The trade-off: against well-prepared GMs, the London is barely an opening — your opponent equalises automatically. But against everyone else (and in bullet against EVERYONE), it's a tempo factory. Hikaru's blitz/bullet rating is partly built on this opening.
keyIdeas: Bf4 BEFORE e3 — non-negotiable, every time. If you play e3 first, the bishop is locked behind its own pawn permanently. This is the single rule you can't break in the London.
Handle …Qb6 with Qb3 (offering the queen trade), NOT Qc1 (passive). The Qb3 forces Black to choose: trade queens into a slightly worse endgame OR retreat the queen losing a tempo.

The Bf4-Bg3 retreat after …Nh5 attacks is the bishop's safety move. Don't trade Bf4 for the knight just because Black offers; retreat to g3 where the bishop keeps its diagonal AND the e2-pawn defends it from further harassment.

Don't play e4 for 20+ moves. The London works because of patient piece improvement: Bd3 → eventually swing the rook to e1 → maybe Ne5 outpost → THEN consider e4 break once Black has run out of useful moves.
traps: Against …Qb6 hitting b2, the immediate Qb3! is the answer. Black has a tactical problem: if they trade queens with Qxb3, axb3 gives White an open a-file AND the b3-pawn defends c4 perfectly; if they retreat the queen, they've lost two tempos for nothing.
warnings: DON'T play e3 before Bf4 — bishop trapped forever.
DON'T overextend the kingside without securing the centre. Premature h4 or g4 in the London exposes your king when Black still has the …c5 break ready to crack the centre open.

variations.name: Standard vs d5
variations.pgn: d4 d5 Nf3 Nf6 Bf4 c5 e3 Nc6 c3 Qb6 Qb3 c4 Qc2 Bf5 Qc1 e6 Nbd2 Be7 Be2 O-O O-O
variations.explanation: The London System against …d5, with Hikaru's specific move-order. After 5.e3 (NOT before Bf4!) Nc6 6.c3 Qb6 (Black hits b2) 7.Qb3! (the standard counter — offers queen trade) c4 (Black declines the trade by pushing the c-pawn) 8.Qc2 Bf5 (Black develops to a useful square) 9.Qc1 (the queen retreats to a safe square) e6 10.Nbd2 Be7 11.Be2 O-O 12.O-O — full development reached. The position is balanced; the London has done its job by avoiding theory and reaching a playable middlegame. From here it's pure technique.

variations.name: vs KID Setup
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Nf3 g6 Bf4 Bg7 e3 O-O Be2 d6 h3 Nbd7 O-O c5 c3 b6 Nbd2 Bb7 a4 a6
variations.explanation: When Black plays the fianchetto with 2...g6 + 3...Bg7, the London adapts. Hikaru uses 4.e3 O-O 5.Be2 (modest setup — the Bd3 attacking idea doesn't work against a fianchettoed king) d6 6.h3! (prophylaxis against …Nh5 trying to trade the f4-bishop). After natural development 7.O-O Nbd7 8.c3 c5 9.Nbd2 b6 10.Bb7 11.a4 a6 — both sides have completed development. The position is solid; Hikaru wins these games through superior middlegame play, not opening tricks.

openings.name: Sicilian Najdorf (Hikaru)
openings.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be3 e5 Nb3 Be6 f3 Be7 Qd2 O-O O-O-O Nbd7 g4 b5
openings.overview: Where Carlsen plays the Najdorf to draw, Hikaru plays it to win. His Najdorf is the polar opposite of Magnus's — sharp English Attack lines, opposite-side castling, pawn races to the death. Hikaru's calculation speed in bullet/blitz makes him deadly in these positions: while opponents are working out the b5-b4 timing, he's already calculated the entire forced sequence. The Najdorf in Hikaru's hands is a knife fight — and he's the fastest knife in chess.
keyIdeas: …a6 + …e5 + …Be6 + …Be7 + …Nbd7 + …O-O is the standard English Attack development sequence. Memorise this seven-move pattern; it's the same in 90% of Hikaru's Najdorf games.
When White castles long (after Be3 + Qd2 + O-O-O), DON'T castle short reflexively. Play …b5 first STARTING your own attack, only castle once White has committed to the kingside pawn storm.

…b5–b4 is the queenside breakthrough. The b4 push forces White's Nc3 to move (to a4, b1, or sacrifices on d5) — every White response creates problems. Push …b5 with one tempo to spare before White's g5 lands.

Watch for the Nd5 sacrifice constantly. In English Attack lines, the typical Nd5 sac (Nxd5 exd5 + e5 push) breaks open Black's centre. Defend by removing the d5-knight with …Nxd5 OR by not allowing the e5 push afterwards.
traps: After g4 (the typical English Attack push), DON'T panic and play defensive moves. Play …b5 attacking on your wing. The race favours the side who attacks faster, and …b5 starts the queenside attack one move earlier than White's g5 can land.
warnings: …b5 timing matters. If played BEFORE the queenside knight develops (…Nbd7), the b5-pawn is undefended and Na5 + Bxb5 can win material. The right order: complete development first, …b5 second.
Nd5 sacrifices appear in almost every English Attack game. If White's Nc3 looks 'parked' and a Nd5! move is in the air, ALWAYS calculate it before making your move.

variations.name: English Attack 6.Be3
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be3 e5 Nb3 Be6 f3 Be7 Qd2 O-O O-O-O Nbd7 g4 b5
variations.explanation: Hikaru's main Najdorf line — opposite-side castling race. After the standard development 6.Be3 e5 7.Nb3 Be6 8.f3 Be7 9.Qd2 O-O 10.O-O-O — White's king is on c1, Black's on g8. White launches with 10...Nbd7 11.g4 (the attacking signal) and Black immediately responds 11...b5! starting their own attack. From here it's a pure pawn-race middlegame: White pushes g4-g5 to attack g7, Black pushes b5-b4 to attack c3. Hikaru's calculation speed in bullet means he typically reaches the critical position 10-15 seconds ahead on the clock, and that advantage compounds into wins.

variations.name: 6.Bg5 Classical
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Bg5 e6 f4 Be7 Qf3 Qc7 O-O-O Nbd7 Bd3 b5 Rhe1 Bb7
variations.explanation: Against the classical 6.Bg5 system, Hikaru plays the standard …e6 setup (NOT the Poisoned Pawn — that's the other Bg5 variation). After 6...e6 7.f4 Be7 8.Qf3 Qc7 9.O-O-O Nbd7 10.Bd3 b5 11.Rhe1 Bb7 — both sides have fully developed for the opposite-side race. The …Bb7 fianchetto is the key piece — it aims at the white king on c1. Hikaru's tactical sense in these positions is legendary.

variations.name: Poisoned Pawn 6.Bg5 Qb6
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Bg5 e6 f4 Qb6 Qd2 Qxb2 Rb1 Qa3 e5 dxe5 fxe5 Nfd7 Ne4 h6 Bh4 Qxa2 Rd1 Qd5
variations.explanation: The most theoretical line in chess — Fischer's pet weapon, revived by every theory hound including Hikaru. Black grabs the b2-pawn with the queen (…Qxb2), then runs the queen all over the board collecting pawns: …Qa3 (only square avoiding Nb5), then …Qxa2 grabbing a second pawn, then …Qd5 reaching safety. Black ends up TWO pawns ahead in a defensible position — IF you know every forced move. ONE wrong move is mate in 5. Hikaru's calculation depth makes this his secret weapon when he needs full points.

openings.name: King's Indian Defense (Hikaru)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 Nd3 f5 f3 f4
openings.overview: Hikaru's KID is built on the principle: 'White's queenside attack matters less than my kingside attack.' He embraces the locked centre, accepts the d5 closure, then launches the pawn storm with violence. His blitz/bullet KID games end in spectacular kingside attacks against opponents who never quite mobilise their queenside in time. The opening is theoretically risky — engines slightly prefer White — but practically the KID rewards calculation speed, and nobody calculates faster than Hikaru in blitz.
keyIdeas: The Bg7 controls everything dark on the long diagonal. Don't trade it for anything less than a winning advantage; the Bg7 is the heart of the KID structure both as defender (covers king's dark squares) and attacker (eyes d4 and the queenside).

After …e5 → d5 closes the centre, the game splits in two: White attacks queenside (c4-c5, b4-b5, a4), Black attacks kingside (f5-f4-g5, Nh5-f4, h-pawn push). The faster attack wins — and Black's attack is usually faster because …f5 doesn't require a-pawn preparation.

f5 → f4 → g5 → g4 is the standard kingside pawn cascade. Don't break this sequence — every move has its purpose: f5 cracks White's centre, f4 seals it, g5 prepares g4, g4 cracks White's king position open.

Knight rerouting is the KID's secret. Nf6-h5-f4 (or Nf6-e8-g7-f5-f4) lands the knight on the attacking square f4 from where it dominates White's defence. Memorise these knight tours — they're 80% of the KID's winning plans.
traps: After 7.d5 Ne7 (the standard knight retreat), the knight reroutes via 9.Ne1 → 10.Nd3 (White's knight tour) to support the queenside attack. Don't let White complete this; play …f5 IMMEDIATELY to start the kingside cascade.
warnings: If White plays c5 before you've completed kingside development, the c-file opens for White's rooks and your queenside collapses before your attack matures. Always have …a5 or …dxc5 ready when White pushes c5.
DON'T play …f5 if White can respond exf5 opening the centre. Wait for the centre to lock with d5 first — the d5-pawn is the lock that makes …f5 safe.

variations.name: Classical Main Line
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 Nd3 f5 f3 f4
variations.explanation: The Mar del Plata pattern in its purest form. After both sides reach the locked centre with 7.O-O Nc6 8.d5 Ne7 — pause and note: White's plan is queenside expansion (b4, a4, eventually c5); Black's plan is kingside attack (f5-f4-g5). Both sides reroute their knights: White's Nf3 goes to Ne1 → Nd3 supporting the queenside; Black's Nf6 goes to Nd7 → eventually Ng7 → Nf5. After 11.Nd3 f5 12.f3 (White slows the attack) f4! — the kingside is now LOCKED with Black's pawns one rank ahead. The …g5 push is next, then …h5, then …Rf6 lift. The KID at its most violent.

variations.name: Saemisch Variation
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 f3 O-O Be3 e5 d5 Nh5 Qd2 f5 O-O-O Nd7 Bd3 Nc5 Bc2 a5
variations.explanation: Against the Saemisch (5.f3) — White's most dangerous KID weapon because the f3-pawn supports both the centre AND a g4 push (cramping the …f5-knight). Black's plan flips: play …Nh5! immediately (the knight reroutes to f4 or g3 sacrificing structure for activity), 7.Qd2 f5! (commit to the attack), 8.O-O-O (White opposite-castles too) Nd7 — both kings exposed, race on. The …Nc5 + …a5 prepares the queenside attack against the white king on c1. The Saemisch positions are some of the sharpest in chess.

variations.name: Bayonet Attack 9.b4
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 b4 Nh5 Re1 f5 Ng5 Nf6 Bf3 c6
variations.explanation: The Bayonet Attack with 9.b4 — White's most aggressive Anti-KID. Black has to respond accurately: 9...Nh5! (preparing …f5 immediately, NOT waiting for the slow knight reroute) 10.Re1 f5 — Black gets …f5 in with one tempo to spare. White's 11.Ng5 tries to trade off the f5-knight area; Black plays 11...Nf6 preserving the attacking knight. The …c6 break challenges White's centre. Razor-sharp position where the better-prepared side usually wins.

openings.name: Nimzo-Indian Defense (Hikaru)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Qc2 O-O a3 Bxc3+ Qxc3 d5 Nf3 dxc4 Qxc4 b6 Bg5 Ba6 Qc2 Nbd7 e3
openings.overview: Aron Nimzowitsch — the Latvian-Danish chess revolutionary whose 1925 book 'My System' redefined chess strategy — invented the Nimzo-Indian to prove that pinning a knight is worth more than developing a centre. Capablanca dismissed Nimzowitsch's hypermodern ideas at first, then started losing tournaments to them. Within 20 years the Nimzo-Indian was Black's main response to 1.d4 at the elite level. Karpov used it extensively, Kasparov fought against it in his Karpov matches, and modern World Championship matches feature Nimzo battles in every iteration. The 3.Nc3 Bb4 pin doesn't just attack the c3-knight; it prevents White's natural e4 push entirely. Without e4, White's centre stays modest, Black gets time to develop, and the structural imbalances that arise (Black with doubled c-pawns for White vs Black with the bishop pair for White) create rich middlegame positions that reward understanding.
keyIdeas: …Bb4 pins more than the knight — it prevents White's entire e4 plan. As long as the bishop sits on b4, e4 hangs to …Bxc3+, leaving White's centre permanently restricted.
After …Bxc3+ bxc3 (or Qxc3), Black has TWO structural advantages: White's doubled c-pawns are a long-term target, AND Black gets to play …c5/…d5 freely. The cost: Black gave up the bishop pair.

…b6 + …Ba6 is the Nimzo's signature manoeuvre. The light-squared bishop comes out via a6 (NOT b7), hitting White's c4-pawn AND eyeing the long diagonal. Combined with …Nbd7 the position is fully mobilised.
Time the …c5 break carefully. Played too early, the d4-pawn becomes too strong; played correctly (after White has committed the dark-squared bishop), …c5 cracks White's centre open.
traps: Against White's 4.f3 (the Sämisch Nimzo), the f3-pawn supports e4 BUT weakens the kingside. Black plays …d5 hitting the centre and the weakened e3-square becomes a permanent home for Black's pieces.
