warnings: DON'T let White play e4 unchallenged. Whenever White prepares e4 (with Qc2, f3, or Bd3), play …d5 immediately to challenge the centre — letting e4 land gives White a winning structural advantage.

Against 4.a3 (the Sämisch — yes, also called Sämisch), accept the …Bxc3 trade ONLY if you can win the c-pawn structure. If White recaptures Qxc3 keeping the structure intact, you've given up the bishop for nothing.

variations.name: Classical 4.Qc2
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Qc2 O-O a3 Bxc3+ Qxc3 d5 Nf3 dxc4 Qxc4 b6 Bg5 Ba6 Qc2 Nbd7 e3
variations.explanation: The Capablanca Variation — White avoids the structural concession by recapturing with the queen (Qxc3). After 4.Qc2 O-O 5.a3 Bxc3+ 6.Qxc3 d5 — White has the bishop pair but no doubled pawns; Black challenges the centre immediately. The 7.Nf3 dxc4 8.Qxc4 sequence trades White's c-pawn for time. Black's plan: 8...b6 9.Bg5 Ba6! (the bishop comes to a6 hitting c4 indirectly) 10.Qc2 Nbd7 11.e3 — full development reached. The position is balanced; the strategic battle is whether White's bishop pair compensates Black's central control.

variations.name: Rubinstein 4.e3
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 e3 O-O Bd3 d5 Nf3 c5 O-O dxc4 Bxc4 Nbd7 Qe2 b6 Rd1 Bb7
variations.explanation: The Rubinstein System — White plays the modest 4.e3 keeping options open. After 4...O-O 5.Bd3 d5 (Black challenges the centre immediately) 6.Nf3 c5 (the equalising break) 7.O-O dxc4 8.Bxc4 Nbd7 9.Qe2 b6 10.Rd1 Bb7 — both sides develop naturally. The …Bb7 fianchetto is preferred over …Ba6 here because the bishop has more activity on the long diagonal. Position is equal but with chances for both sides to outplay.

openings.name: Benko Gambit (Hikaru)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 g6 g3 Bg7 Kg2 O-O Nf3 Nbd7
openings.overview: Hikaru's Benko Gambit is the most positionally sound gambit ever invented. Named after Hungarian-American GM Pal Benko, who defected from Hungary in 1957 and spent his career championing the gambit before formally publishing his Benko Gambit book in 1974, the opening became serious tournament theory in the 1970s-1980s. Topalov and Karpov played both sides; in modern chess it remains a respected weapon when Black needs winning chances against 1.d4. Black sacrifices the b5-pawn for ETERNAL pressure: the a-file opens for the rook, the b-file opens for another rook, the Bg7 fianchetto stares down the long diagonal at White's queenside, and the structural compensation never goes away — even in pawn endgames 60 moves later. Hikaru is a Benko specialist; in his hands, the gambit becomes a slow strangulation where White's extra pawn never matters because Black's pieces are always more active.
keyIdeas: The b5-pawn sacrifice opens BOTH the a-file AND the b-file. After cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6, the queenside structure is fixed: White has b/c-pawns isolated and weak; Black has the open files for both rooks.

Bxa6-Bxf1 is the forced trade. After …Bxa6 attacking c4 (which doesn't exist — it's the f1-bishop the bishop targets via the diagonal), White must trade with Bxa6 OR Kxf1 — and Kxf1 ruins White's castling permanently.

Bg7 fianchetto is the long-diagonal sniper. It supports the queenside attack indirectly (aimed at b2, blocked only by White's queenside knight) AND provides defensive support for the kingside if needed.

The endgame is the Benko's secret strength. Most gambits lose their potency once queens trade; the Benko's open files and active rooks become MORE valuable in endgames, not less. Don't fear queen trades.
traps: If White tries to KEEP the a6-pawn (instead of recapturing with bxa6), Black plays …Bxa6 anyway — the bishop on a6 hits BOTH c4 AND the f1-bishop. White is forced to trade with Bxa6 Nxa6 simplifying into Benko-style compensation, OR worse, accept Bxf1 ruining the castling rights.
warnings: Don't play the Benko if White declines with 4.Nf3 (instead of 4.cxb5). The declined Benko transposes into a Benoni structure where you don't get the open queenside files. Switch openings or accept the position.
Keep pieces ACTIVE in the endgame. If you let White trade off your active pieces, the structural compensation evaporates. The Benko's edge is dynamic; trade only when it improves your activity.

variations.name: Accepted Main Line
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 g6 g3 Bg7 Kg2 O-O Nf3 Nbd7
variations.explanation: The main Benko Accepted position with the king walk. After 5.bxa6 Bxa6 6.Nc3 d6 7.e4 Bxf1! (the bishop voluntarily trades on f1 because White's only defence Nxf1 loses to …Bxc4 etc.) 8.Kxf1 — White's king has lost castling. 8...g6 9.g3 (preparing the king walk to g2) Bg7 10.Kg2 O-O 11.Nf3 Nbd7. Final position: White's king on g2 is uncomfortable, the a/b-files are open, the Bg7 is a permanent sniper. White has the extra pawn (the a6-pawn captured earlier); Black has the structural and piece activity. The compensation lasts the entire game.

variations.name: Fianchetto Declined
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 g6 Nc3 Bxa6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 d6 g3 Bg7 Kg2 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Re1 Qa5
variations.explanation: An alternative move order — Black plays …g6 (fianchetto first) BEFORE recapturing on a6 with the bishop. The result is similar but the timing gives Black more options. After 6.Nc3 Bxa6 (NOW the bishop comes out) 7.e4 Bxf1 8.Kxf1 d6 — same king-walk position reached. Hikaru sometimes uses this move order to confuse opponents' preparation; the …Qa5 + …Nbd7 + (later) …Rfb8 plan is identical to the main line.

variations.name: King Walk with a4
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 c5 d5 b5 cxb5 a6 bxa6 Bxa6 Nc3 d6 e4 Bxf1 Kxf1 g6 Nf3 Bg7 g3 O-O Kg2 Nbd7 a4 Qa5 Re1 Rfb8
variations.explanation: When White plays a4 trying to lock the queenside, Black responds with the standard Benko plan: …Qa5 (the queen reaches the perfect attacking square — hits a4 from the queenside) and …Rfb8 (rook to the open b-file). After 13.Re1 Rfb8 — both Black rooks are on the open queenside files, the queen supports the attack from a5, the Bg7 eyes the long diagonal. White has the extra pawn but the entire queenside is under attack from FOUR Black pieces.

openings.name: English Opening (Hikaru)
openings.pgn: c4 e5 Nc3 Nf6 Nf3 Nc6 g3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 Bg2 Nb6 O-O Be7 d3 O-O Be3 Be6 Rc1 f6
openings.overview: Hikaru's English Opening is the chess equivalent of asking 'what's your plan?' before committing to your own. The opening is named for Howard Staunton — the English master who used 1.c4 in his 1843 match against Saint-Amant (the first international chess match in history); Mikhail Botvinnik adopted it as a championship weapon in 1948, and Bobby Fischer famously switched to 1.c4 in critical games of his 1972 World Championship match against Spassky (Game 6 was a celebrated English Opening win). 1.c4 develops nothing — it just claims the d5-square. Black has to commit FIRST to a setup; once they do, you copy or counter accordingly. This is bullet/blitz gold because most opponents have memorised replies to 1.e4 and 1.d4, but almost none have replies prepared against 1.c4.
keyIdeas: c4 controls d5 without committing the centre. Whatever Black plays (…e5, …c5, …Nf6, …e6, …g6), you have a setup ready. The flexibility is the point.
The g3+Bg2 fianchetto is the English's most natural development. The bishop on g2 supports the d5-square (locked down by c4) AND eyes the long diagonal.
When Black plays …e5, you're in a Sicilian a tempo up — use that tempo to push the queenside attack (b4 + a3 + Rb1) faster than Black can defend.
Don't commit to a central pawn structure in the first 10 moves. The English's strategic weapon is FLEXIBILITY — keep d3/d4/e3/e4 all in your pocket until Black commits.
traps: After 1.c4 e5 2.Nc3 Nf6, if Black plays the premature 3.Nf3 d5? (pushing the d-pawn before securing the centre) — you play 4.cxd5 Nxd5 5.Nxd5 Qxd5 6.e3 (preventing …Bf5 or other queen escape) Bc4 hitting the queen with development. Pure tempo theft.
warnings: Don't let Black establish a Maroczy-like centre with …d5 + …e5 unchallenged. If Black gets both pawns to the 5th rank, your structural weapons (g2-bishop pressure, c4 controlling d5) become irrelevant.
The English can become PASSIVE if you play too slowly on the queenside. After the standard fianchetto setup, play b4 with purpose — don't just wait for Black to make a mistake.

variations.name: Reversed Sicilian
variations.pgn: c4 e5 Nc3 Nf6 Nf3 Nc6 g3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 Bg2 Nb6 O-O Be7 d3 O-O Be3 Be6 Rc1 f6
variations.explanation: When Black plays 1...e5, you're now playing a Sicilian as White with an extra tempo. After 2.Nc3 Nf6 3.Nf3 Nc6 4.g3 d5 5.cxd5 Nxd5 (Black's d-knight reaches d5 — but where does it go next?) 6.Bg2 Nb6 (the knight retreats) 7.O-O Be7 8.d3 O-O 9.Be3 Be6 10.Rc1 f6 — White has reached a Dragon-style position but with the extra tempo of d3 already played. The plan: Qd2, Nd5 manoeuvre, eventual b4 expansion. The extra tempo means White's attacking ideas arrive one move earlier than in a regular Open Sicilian.

variations.name: Symmetrical English
variations.pgn: c4 c5 Nc3 Nc6 g3 g6 Bg2 Bg7 Nf3 Nf6 O-O O-O d3 d6 a3 a6 Rb1 Rb8 b4 cxb4 axb4 b5
variations.explanation: When Black copies with 1...c5, both sides fianchetto in mirror image. The position is symmetric until move 10 — and the first side to break the symmetry wins. White plays 11.Rb1 (preparing b4) and 12.b4! cracking the symmetry first. Black's natural 12...cxb4 13.axb4 b5 — Black creates queenside complications but White has the open a-file AND the c-file is now half-open for White's rook. The symmetric structure favours the breaker; that's White.

variations.name: vs King's Indian Setup
variations.pgn: c4 e5 Nc3 Nc6 g3 g6 Bg2 Bg7 d3 d6 Nf3 f5 O-O Nf6 Rb1 O-O b4 h6 b5 Ne7 Ba3 Be6 Nd2
variations.explanation: When Black plays the King's Indian Defence reversed (1...e5 + …g6 + …Bg7), the English's queenside attack becomes especially effective. After 6.d3 d6 7.Nf3 f5 8.O-O Nf6 9.Rb1 O-O (Black castles into your queenside attack!) 10.b4! h6 11.b5 Ne7 12.Ba3 Be6 13.Nd2 — White's queenside attack races against Black's typical KID kingside attack. Critical difference: White has the extra tempo, so the b5 push arrives BEFORE Black's …f4 push. The reversed-KID positions favour White.

openings.name: Ruy Lopez Anti-Marshall (Caruana)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O c3 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Rxe5 c6 d4 Bd6 Re1 Qh4 g3 Qh3
openings.overview: Caruana plays the Ruy Lopez Anti-Marshall because he's spent more hours analysing the Marshall than any human alive. The Marshall Attack itself was unveiled by American champion Frank Marshall in his 1918 New York game against Capablanca — Marshall had supposedly prepared the gambit for ten years specifically to use against Cuba's chess king (Capablanca won that game, but the opening entered chess history). The Anti-Marshall systems (8.a4, 8.h3) were developed in the 1980s-1990s as White's defensive response. Caruana plays both sides at elite level — in the 2018 World Championship match against Carlsen, this opening appeared in 4 of 12 classical games. His Anti-Marshall preparation is so deep that even Magnus avoided the most theoretical lines. When Caruana plays White and Black plays the Marshall, you're watching one of chess history's most prepared theoretical positions reach the board.
keyIdeas: If Black plays the Marshall Attack (…d5 instead of …d6), you have ONE choice to make on move 8: play h3 preventing the Marshall (8.h3 — adds a tempo for safety) OR play 8.a4 sidestepping the Marshall entirely. Both are sound; pick based on your preparation.
If you accept the Marshall (8.c3 d5 9.exd5 Nxd5 10.Nxe5 Nxe5 11.Rxe5 c6), the position is forced for 15 moves. Memorise the defensive moves — every move has only ONE correct answer.

In the Anti-Marshall 8.a4, the goal is positional: probe Black's queenside with a4 forcing …b4, then play d3 + Nbd2 + Bb3 reaching a slow strategic position. Same Ruy Lopez middlegame, NO Marshall risk.
The Breyer Variation (with the …Nb8-Nd7 reroute) is another Anti-Marshall — Black's plan is too slow to support a Marshall gambit. Caruana's deep prep handles both Breyer and Anti-Marshall systems.
traps: Against the Marshall main line (after 11.Rxe5 c6 12.d4), DON'T play 12.dxc6? — losing the c6-pawn isn't worth it. The correct 12.d4 immediately fortifies the centre and prepares the defensive setup (Be3, Nd2, h3) that holds the extra pawn.
warnings: DON'T accept the Marshall without deep preparation. The defensive lines are forced for 20+ moves; one wrong move loses the pawn back AND the game. If you haven't memorised the Be3/Nd2/h3 setup, play the Anti-Marshall (8.a4) instead.
Even small move-order changes matter. The Marshall accepted requires precise sequence: 9.exd5 (NOT 9.Nxe5 first), 10.Nxe5 Nxe5 11.Rxe5 (NOT Nxd5), 12.d4 (NOT Nd2 first). Deviation = losing.

variations.name: Marshall Attack Main Line
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O c3 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Rxe5 c6 d4 Bd6 Re1 Qh4 g3 Qh3
variations.explanation: The Marshall Attack accepted. Black sacrifices a pawn with 8...d5! and after the forced sequence 9.exd5 Nxd5 10.Nxe5 Nxe5 11.Rxe5 c6, White MUST play 12.d4 fortifying the centre. Black continues 12...Bd6 13.Re1 Qh4 14.g3 Qh3 — the queen on h3 is the Marshall's signature attacking move, eyeing g2 and h2. White's defensive task: prevent the attack from breaking through while consolidating the extra pawn. The lines are 25+ moves deep, fully analysed. Caruana wins these as White because he knows ONE more move of theory than the average elite GM.

variations.name: Anti-Marshall 8.a4
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O a4 b4 d3 d6 Nbd2 Na5 Ba2 c5 c3 Rb8
variations.explanation: Caruana's chess-club-safe Anti-Marshall. 8.a4 probes Black's queenside; after 8...b4 (Black closes the queenside) 9.d3 (modest, keeps options) d6 10.Nbd2 Na5 (kicking the b3-bishop) 11.Ba2 c5 12.c3 Rb8 — both sides have completed development for a slow positional middlegame. The position avoids ALL Marshall tactics; Caruana just plays for the slight space edge with standard Ruy Lopez methods (h3, Nf1-g3, eventual b4 expansion).

variations.name: Breyer Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 d6 c3 O-O h3 Nb8 d4 Nbd7 Nbd2 Bb7 Bc2 Re8 Nf1 Bf8
variations.explanation: The Breyer Defence (with 9...Nb8) — Black avoids the Marshall by playing the conservative …d6 first. After 8.h3 (prophylaxis) Nb8! (the mysterious knight retreat) 9.d4 Nbd7 (the knight reroutes via d7) — Black has reached a flexible structure. White continues 10.Nbd2 Bb7 11.Bc2 Re8 12.Nf1 Bf8 — both sides regroup for the long strategic battle. The Breyer is the world's deepest opening at GM level; Caruana's preparation here is the deepest of anyone's.

openings.name: Giuoco Piano (Caruana)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 cxd4 Bb4+ Bd2 Bxd2+ Nbxd2 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Qb3 Nce7 O-O O-O Rfe1 c6
openings.overview: Caruana's Italian is what happens when a former World Championship challenger spends ten years analysing one opening. The Giuoco Piano traces back to Pedro Damiano's 1512 chess treatise and Greco's 1620 manuscripts; Paul Morphy's mid-1800s European tour included devastating Italian games, and the IQP structure that arises after c3+d4 was systematized by Tarrasch (whose 1909 book 'Die moderne Schachpartie' is one of chess's foundational texts). Caruana plays the c3+d4 version because the resulting middlegame positions reward precise calculation over creativity — and Caruana calculates better than almost anyone alive. His Italian win-rate at the top level is exceptional. The trick: every line resolves into ONE of three structures (IQP, hanging pawns, or symmetrical), and Caruana knows each one to the endgame.
keyIdeas: The c3+d4 push is the entire opening's engine. After 4.c3 Nf6 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4, you've reached the IQP structure — accept the isolated d-pawn for the piece activity it generates.

Qb3 is Caruana's signature move. Placed on b3 the queen creates DOUBLE pressure: hits b7 (defended only by Nb8 if developed) AND defends c4 (sometimes vulnerable). The …Nce7 retreat to defend b7 is forced.
Keep pieces on the board in the IQP middlegame. The d4-pawn is a strength while attacking; trade pieces and it becomes a structural target in the endgame. Trade only when ahead in development.

Caruana's preparation depth is the real opening. Every IQP variation has been analysed by his team for years; he reaches the resulting middlegame faster than any opponent and the technical edge wins games.
traps: After 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 Bb4+ 7.Bd2 Bxd2+ 8.Nbxd2 d5 9.exd5 Nxd5 10.Qb3! — the queen-pressure on b7 is the position's defining tactical theme. Black's only good move is 10...Nce7 defending b7; ANY other move loses material to Qxb7 or Bxd5 followed by Qxb7.
warnings: DON'T trade queens early. The IQP middlegame favours White WITH queens (attacking chances); it favours Black WITHOUT queens (the IQP becomes a weakness). Caruana keeps queens on until the structural game is decided.
Watch for …Nxe4 tactical resources after the central exchanges. If your e4-pawn is undefended (after c3+d4 trades), Black's pieces might find tactics on the central diagonals.

variations.name: Main Line with IQP
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 cxd4 Bb4+ Bd2 Bxd2+ Nbxd2 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Qb3 Nce7 O-O O-O Rfe1 c6
variations.explanation: Caruana's flagship Italian IQP line. The forced sequence: 4.c3 Nf6 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 Bb4+ (the standard check) 7.Bd2 Bxd2+ 8.Nbxd2 d5 9.exd5 Nxd5 10.Qb3! — the signature move. Black's c6-knight is now under attack indirectly (the b7-pawn is the real target). After 10...Nce7 (defending b7, forced) 11.O-O O-O 12.Rfe1 c6 — full development reached. White has the isolated d4-pawn AND the queen-on-b3 pressure; Black has solid defence but no active counterplay. Caruana wins by squeezing the IQP middlegame with superior technique.

variations.name: Slow Italian with d3
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d3 d6 O-O a6 a4 Ba7 Nbd2 O-O Re1 Be6 Bb3 h6
variations.explanation: When Caruana wants a slower game (or his preparation handles the d3 system better against a specific opponent), he plays 4.d3 keeping the central tension closed. After 4...d6 5.O-O a6 6.a4 Ba7 7.Nbd2 O-O 8.Re1 Be6 9.Bb3 h6 — both sides reach a slow positional position. Plan from here: the standard Italian middlegame with Nf1-g3 reroute, h3 prophylaxis, eventual d4 break when conditions are ripe. Same opening, slower tempo.

openings.name: Anti-Berlin (Caruana)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 d3 Bc5 Bxc6 dxc6 Nbd2 Be6 O-O Bd6 Nb3 Qe7
openings.overview: Caruana's Anti-Berlin is the answer to the Berlin's biggest problem: it's a draw. The d3 Anti-Berlin (sometimes called the 'Modern Steinitz' or 'Bishop Defence Deferred') gained popularity at the elite level in the 2010s specifically as a response to Kramnik's 2000 Berlin Wall victory over Kasparov — top players needed a way to keep queens on the board against the Berlin Defence. Caruana and Anand were among the early adopters; today every top player has 4.d3 prepared as an alternative to the queenless endgame. The Berlin Endgame after Qxd8+ has a 60%+ drawing rate at elite level — too high for someone who needs wins. So Caruana plays 4.d3 instead of 4.O-O, AVOIDING the queen trade entirely. The result: a middlegame Ruy Lopez where Black has the doubled c-pawns (from Bxc6 trade) but keeps the queens on, and White has the bishop pair plus all the practical winning chances.
keyIdeas: 4.d3 is the entire Anti-Berlin idea. By playing d3 BEFORE Black can grab e4 with …Nxe4, the queen trade is impossible — Black has to play a middlegame.
Bxc6 + the doubled c-pawn structure is the strategic battleground. Black's bishop pair is the only real compensation; you target c6+c7 throughout the middlegame.
Keep queens on the board. The whole point of d3-Anti-Berlin is to AVOID the queenless endgame; trade queens only when ahead in material or structural advantage.
Develop with Nbd2-b3 reroute (instead of Nc3) — the knight reaches the queenside outpost to support pressure on c5/c6.
traps: After 4.d3 Bc5 (Black develops aggressively), 5.Bxc6+! immediately — the doubled c-pawns are permanent AND the c5-bishop is now misplaced (defended only by …Qe7 which is awkward). The structural damage is decisive over 40-50 moves.
warnings: Don't expect a large opening advantage. The Anti-Berlin gives WHITE small positional pluses, not big advantages — your edge comes from the middlegame and Black's natural defensive difficulties.
If Black plays the …Bc5/Bd6/Be7 setup quickly and accurately, the doubled c-pawns become less of a target. Patience is required to convert.

variations.name: d3 Anti-Berlin
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 d3 Bc5 Bxc6 dxc6 Nbd2 Be6 O-O Bd6 Nb3 Qe7
variations.explanation: The full Anti-Berlin. After 4.d3 Bc5 (Black's most natural reply) 5.Bxc6 dxc6 — doubled c-pawns achieved. The 6.Nbd2 development keeps the b3-square free for the knight reroute. After 6...Be6 7.O-O Bd6 (Black reroutes the bishop to a more useful square) 8.Nb3 Qe7 — both sides have completed development. The middlegame battle: White has the bishop pair AND the d3+e4 stable structure; Black has the doubled c-pawns but active piece play. Caruana's preparation in these positions gives him the edge from move 12 onward.

variations.name: 5.O-O Berlin Main Line
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 Nd6 Bxc6 dxc6 dxe5 Nf5 Qxd8+ Kxd8 Nc3 Ke8
variations.explanation: For comparison — the regular Berlin Endgame that Caruana AVOIDS by playing 4.d3 instead of 4.O-O. After 4.O-O Nxe4 5.d4 Nd6 6.Bxc6 dxc6 7.dxe5 Nf5 8.Qxd8+ Kxd8 9.Nc3 Ke8 — the queens are off, the position is technically equal but practically drawn. Caruana ONLY enters this position when he needs a draw (rare); usually he prefers the d3-Anti-Berlin keeping queens on the board.

openings.name: Petroff Defense (Caruana)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 d4 d5 Bd3 Nc6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb4 Be2 O-O Nc3 Bf5 a3 Nxc3 bxc3 Nc6
openings.overview: The Petroff is the Berlin Defence's older brother — it's been Black's drawing weapon for 150 years. Named after Russian master Alexander Petrov (or Petroff in transliteration) who analyzed it in the 1840s, the defence has been a quiet sideline for most of chess history until the 1990s when Vladimir Kramnik began using it as his main weapon at the elite level — Kramnik scored a famous string of Petroff draws and wins against Kasparov. Caruana plays it as his 'absolutely must not lose' opening: against in-form opponents, in match situations, when a draw clinches a tournament victory. The Petroff's structure is symmetric, the resulting positions are almost forced equality, and Caruana's preparation in the rare unbalanced lines is legendary. If you're trying to beat Caruana in the Petroff with White, you're going to need a lot of theory and a lot of luck.
keyIdeas: The symmetric mirror — Black copies White's moves until move 5. After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nf6, you're playing the Petroff. White's choice on move 3 (Nxe5 or d4) determines the structure; Black equalises either way.
After 3.Nxe5 d6 4.Nf3 Nxe4 — the standard equalising sequence. Black has recovered the pawn and developed; the position is symmetric. The …d5 break next move is automatic.
Bishop development order: …Bd6 (active) OR …Be7 (solid) BEFORE …e6 (which locks the c8-bishop in). The Petroff specifically AVOIDS the Caro-Kann's bishop problem by getting the bishops out first.

Caruana's secret: in the lines where White tries to keep an edge (5.Nc3 instead of 5.d4), Caruana has prepared improvements that turn the position from 'slight White edge' to 'equal'. The Petroff has no losing lines for Black if you know the theory.
traps: After 3.Nxe5 d6, NEVER play 4.Nf3 Nxe4 5.d4 d5 6.Nbd2 (instead of 6.Bd3) — Black plays 6...Bf5 with comfortable equality. The correct order is 6.Bd3 first, defending the e4-knight from any tactics.
warnings: The Petroff IS boring — that's the point. If you NEED to play for a win as Black against 1.e4, the Petroff isn't your weapon; play the Najdorf or Sveshnikov instead.

Watch for the Cochrane Gambit (4.Nxf7!? Kxf7) — completely unsound objectively but tactically tricky in fast time controls. Caruana has specific defensive preparation; without it, the king walk is dangerous.

variations.name: Classical 5.d4
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 d4 d5 Bd3 Nc6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb4 Be2 O-O Nc3 Bf5 a3 Nxc3 bxc3 Nc6
variations.explanation: Caruana's main Petroff line. After the textbook 5.d4 d5 (mirror moves) 6.Bd3 Nc6 7.O-O Be7 8.c4 — White challenges the centre with c4, but Black responds 8...Nb4! kicking the bishop. After 9.Be2 O-O 10.Nc3 Bf5 11.a3 Nxc3 12.bxc3 — White has the doubled c-pawns (structural concession) while Black has the active …Bf5 + …Nc6 piece play. The position is equal but with chances for both sides. Caruana converts these into draws or wins through pure technique.

variations.name: 5.Nc3 Main Line
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 Nc3 Nxc3 dxc3 Be7 Bf4 Nd7
variations.explanation: When White plays 5.Nc3 instead of 5.d4 (avoiding the main lines), Black equalises with 5...Nxc3 6.dxc3 — White has doubled c-pawns immediately. After 6...Be7 7.Bf4 Nd7 — symmetric pawn structure, balanced piece play, no realistic winning chances for either side. The Petroff at its most peaceful.

openings.name: Queen's Gambit Declined (Caruana)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Be7 Bf4 O-O e3 Nbd7 Be2 c5 dxc5 Nxc5 O-O dxc4 Bxc4 a6 a4
openings.overview: Caruana's QGD is reliability incarnate. The structure (d5+e6+Nf6+Be7+O-O) is the most battle-tested defensive setup in chess — Steinitz used it in the late 1800s, Tarrasch built his teaching career on it, Capablanca made it his lifelong defence (his 1922 instructional book 'Chess Fundamentals' devotes substantial space to QGD principles), Karpov played it through his entire World Championship era. The Lasker Defence (with …Ne4) is named for World Champion Emanuel Lasker; the Ragozin Variation comes from Soviet GM Vyacheslav Ragozin who pioneered it in the 1930s. Caruana adds modern theoretical depth: he knows the …c5 break timing in EVERY White system, his Lasker preparation handles the Exchange Variation, and his Ragozin alternative gives him a more combative option when he needs winning chances. Three openings in one repertoire — all solid, all prepared.
keyIdeas: The …c5 break is the QGD's equalising lever. Played at the right moment (after both sides develop), …c5 challenges d4 and opens the position. Played too early, White plays d5 closing the centre.

After …c5 dxc5 …Nxc5, the knight reaches the active c5 outpost. This is Caruana's preferred recapture (instead of …Bxc5) because the knight on c5 pressures e4 AND a4 while the bishop stays on e7 supporting the king.
The c8-bishop's escape plan is critical. After the standard …c5/dxc5/Nxc5 + …dxc4/Bxc4, Black plays …a6 (preparing …b5) AND …Bd7 (the bishop heads to b7 via …Bb7).

The Ragozin is the QGD's aggressive alternative. Played with …Bb4 pinning the c3-knight (instead of …Be7), it creates immediate tension and unbalanced positions. Caruana uses it when he needs to win as Black.
traps: If White plays the Exchange Variation (cxd5 exd5) before completing development, Black's IQP-shaped position has the better pieces. The …Bf5 + …Nc6 setup combined with …Nb4 ideas pressures the c2-pawn — White's structural edge becomes Black's piece activity.
warnings: Don't play …c5 if White can answer d5 closing the centre with tempo on your pieces. Wait until the position is open enough that …c5 dxc5 forces White to capture.
Plan for the c8-bishop from move 6. The …Bd7 + …Bd7-Be8-f5 reroute is slow but reliable; the …b6+…Bb7 fianchetto is faster but requires careful timing to avoid weakening c6.

variations.name: 5.Bf4 with c5
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Be7 Bf4 O-O e3 Nbd7 Be2 c5 dxc5 Nxc5 O-O dxc4 Bxc4 a6 a4
variations.explanation: Caruana's main QGD line against the modern 5.Bf4 system. After 5...Be7 6.e3 (NOT 6.cxd5 — White keeps tension) Nbd7 (development) 7.Be2 c5! (the equalising break played EXACTLY on schedule) 8.dxc5 Nxc5 — the d7-knight reaches the active c5 outpost. After 9.O-O dxc4 10.Bxc4 a6 11.a4 (White prevents …b5) — full development reached, position balanced. From here Caruana plays the standard QGD plan: …b6 + …Bb7 + …Rfc8 for queenside pressure.

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 Nbd7 Nf3 Re8 Qc2 c6 O-O Nf8 h3 Be6
variations.explanation: Against the Exchange Variation (cxd5 exd5), Caruana plays the textbook minority attack DEFENCE. The Carlsbad structure (Black has pawns on c6+d5, White on b/c/d-files) is well-known: White plans Rab1 + b4 + b5 attacking c6; Black defends with …a6 + …Rb8 + …Nb6 protecting c6. The 9...Nf8 manoeuvre is the QGD's signature — reroute the knight via f8 to g6 or e6 supporting the …f5 break or the …Bf5 light-square trade.

variations.name: Ragozin Variation
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Bb4 Bg5 h6 Bxf6 Qxf6 e3 O-O Rc1 dxc4 Bxc4 c5 O-O cxd4 Ne4 Qe7 exd4 Nc6
variations.explanation: Caruana's aggressive QGD alternative — the Ragozin with 4...Bb4 pinning the c3-knight. After 5.Bg5 h6 6.Bxf6 Qxf6 — Black has the bishop pair but doubled queen-side. The …c5 break comes early: 9...c5! 10.O-O cxd4 11.Ne4 (active knight reroute) Qe7 12.exd4 Nc6 — Black has equalised material and reached a balanced middlegame with active piece play. The Ragozin gives Caruana fighting chances as Black against opponents who only prepare the main QGD lines.

openings.name: Nimzo-Indian Defense (Caruana)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Qc2 O-O a3 Bxc3+ Qxc3 d5 Nf3 c5 dxc5
openings.overview: Caruana's Nimzo-Indian is the second pillar of his Black repertoire against 1.d4 (alongside the QGD). Created by Aron Nimzowitsch in the 1920s as part of the hypermodern revolution, the Nimzo immediately challenged classical opening theory; Capablanca initially dismissed it but was forced to adopt it after losing to Nimzowitsch in Carlsbad 1929. The 4.Qc2 (Capablanca) system bears the Cuban champion's name; the 4.e3 (Rubinstein) system honours Akiba Rubinstein, the great Polish master. Where the QGD is solid defence, the Nimzo creates structural imbalances that reward calculation. The 3...Bb4 pin doesn't just attack the c3-knight — it prevents e4 forever as long as the bishop stays on b4. Caruana's preparation in the Capablanca and Rubinstein systems is so deep that even Magnus avoids the most theoretical lines.
keyIdeas: …Bb4 is the structural pin. As long as the bishop sits on b4, White can never play e4 cleanly — the pin holds the position open for Black's strategic plans.
After …Bxc3+, the recapture matters: bxc3 (doubled c-pawns for White, structural concession) or Qxc3 (no doubling, but White's queen committed early). Each leads to different middlegame plans.
The …d5 + …c5 breaks together are the Nimzo's strategic weapons. Time them after White commits the dark-squared bishop (Bg5 or Bd2) to crack open the position with active piece play.
Target the doubled c-pawns in the bxc3 lines. The …c5 break + …Rc8 + …Na5/Nc4 reroute pile up on c4; White's bishop pair compensates but the structural target never goes away.
traps: After 4.Qc2 O-O 5.a3 Bxc3+ 6.Qxc3 d5 — Black's immediate central thrust is critical. The c3-queen has to defend the c-pawn (and prepare cxd5 if needed); meanwhile Black completes development with full equality.
warnings: Don't surrender the bishop pair without forcing concessions. If White can play a3 and you …Bxc3+ without White having to recapture bxc3, you've given up the bishop for nothing concrete.
Time …d5 carefully. Played too early (before White commits pieces), …d5 can be met with cxd5 reaching favourable IQP positions for White; played correctly, it's the equalising lever.

variations.name: 4.Qc2 Main Line
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Qc2 O-O a3 Bxc3+ Qxc3 d5 Nf3 c5 dxc5
variations.explanation: The Capablanca Variation — White avoids the doubled c-pawn structure by recapturing with the queen (Qxc3). After 5.a3 Bxc3+ 6.Qxc3 — White has the bishop pair, no structural weaknesses. Black's response: 6...d5 (immediate central challenge) 7.Nf3 c5 (the equalising break) 8.dxc5 — the position simplifies. Black recovers the c5-pawn with …Nbd7 + …Nxc5, reaching a position with the better-coordinated minor pieces vs White's bishop pair. Theoretically balanced, practically a fighting game.

variations.name: 4.e3 Rubinstein
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 e3 O-O Bd3 d5 Nf3 c5 O-O dxc4 Bxc4 cxd4 exd4 b6 Bg5 Bb7 Rc1 Nbd7
variations.explanation: The Rubinstein System with the modest 4.e3. After 4...O-O 5.Bd3 d5 (Black challenges immediately) 6.Nf3 c5 (the equalising break) 7.O-O dxc4 8.Bxc4 cxd4 9.exd4 b6 — Black reaches a Hedgehog-like structure with the bishop pair eliminated but excellent piece coordination. The …Bb7 fianchetto + …Nbd7 + …Rc8 plan creates queenside pressure. Caruana plays this when he wants a strategically rich middlegame without sharp tactics.

openings.name: Catalan Opening (Caruana)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 a4 Bd7 Qxc4 Bc6 Bf4 Bd6 Nc3 Bxf4 gxf4 Nbd7
openings.overview: Where Carlsen plays the Catalan as a positional grinder, Caruana plays it as a precision instrument. Same opening, different temperament. The Catalan was named at the 1929 Barcelona tournament where Savielly Tartakower invented it on the spot for the organizers; Botvinnik and later Kramnik elevated it to championship status; today every elite player has Catalan preparation. Caruana's Catalan preparation extends 25+ moves into specific variations; he reaches the resulting middlegame positions faster than any opponent and the technical edge converts into wins. The Bg2 fianchetto bishop creates the long-term pressure, but it's Caruana's depth of preparation that makes the Catalan deadly in his hands.
keyIdeas: The Bg2 is the opening's defining piece. From g2 it aims at the a8-rook from across the board AND supports queenside pawn pushes. Never trade it without forcing concessions.
After …dxc4 (the Open Catalan), don't rush to recapture — play Qc2 first, then a4 preventing …b5, THEN Qxc4 with full development advantage.

Use the bishop pair AND open files together. The Catalan often leads to positions where you have the bishop pair (after exchanges) AND open queenside files (after a4-a5 or c-file opening). Both factors compound.

Caruana's preparation is the real opening. Every line resolves into one of 4-5 known middlegame structures, all analysed by his team for years. He reaches the critical position 5-10 minutes ahead on the clock.
traps: If Black tries to hold the c4-pawn with …b5, you play a4 immediately undermining the chain. After …c6 (defending b5) axb5 cxb5 — Black's queenside is wrecked AND the Bg2 fires down the open diagonal. Greedy …b5-protection costs material.
warnings: DON'T rush to recover the c4-pawn. Pressure is more valuable than the pawn itself — play Qc2 + a4 setting up the recovery rather than immediate Qxc4 which loses time.

Watch Black's …c5 break. In several Catalan lines (especially after …Bb7 + …Nbd7), the …c5 break opens the centre and dissolves White's structural edge. Play prophylactic moves (a4, b3) to delay this break.

variations.name: Open Catalan with Qc2
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 a4 Bd7 Qxc4 Bc6 Bf4 Bd6 Nc3 Bxf4 gxf4 Nbd7
variations.explanation: Caruana's flagship Catalan. After 6.O-O dxc4 (Open Catalan — Black grabs the pawn) 7.Qc2 (NOT Qxc4 immediately — prepare first) a6 8.a4 (preventing …b5 forever) Bd7 9.Qxc4 (NOW recover the pawn) Bc6 10.Bf4 (active bishop development) Bd6 (Black trades the dark-squared bishops) 11.Nc3 Bxf4 12.gxf4 — doubled f-pawns for White but the g-file opens for the rook lift AND the Bg2 still controls the diagonal. The position favours White's coordinated pieces; Caruana converts these into wins through pure technique.

variations.name: Closed Catalan
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O Nbd7 Qc2 c6 Nbd2 b6 e4 Bb7 e5 Ne8 Re1 Nc7
variations.explanation: In the Closed Catalan, Black plays the safe 5...Nbd7 (instead of …dxc4) keeping the d5-pawn supported. After 6.Qc2 c6 7.Nbd2 b6 (preparing …Bb7 fianchetto) 8.e4! (Caruana's preferred plan — NOW e4 makes sense because Black has committed pieces) Bb7 9.e5 Ne8 (the knight is cramped back) 10.Re1 Nc7 — White has a long-term space advantage. Black's pieces are bottled up; White slowly improves position with no risk.

variations.name: Early Bd7 System
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2 Nbd7
variations.explanation: The modern Black response — after Caruana's standard 6...dxc4 7.Qc2 a6 sequence, Black sometimes plays 8...b5 directly (skipping the …Bd7 prep). After 9.Qc2 (the queen retreats — White accepts the slight tempo loss) Bb7 10.Bd2 Nbd7 — Black has reached an active queenside setup. White's advantage is reduced; the position becomes a strategic battle where Caruana's preparation depth still gives him the edge.

openings.name: Berlin Defense (Caruana)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 Nd6 Bxc6 dxc6 dxe5 Nf5 Qxd8+ Kxd8 Nc3 Ke8 h3 Be7 Bf4 Be6 Rad1 h6
openings.overview: Caruana's Berlin Defence is the world's most prepared draw-weapon. The defence was documented in Siegbert Tarrasch's 1880s analyses but was considered second-tier until Vladimir Kramnik used it to break Kasparov in their 2000 World Championship match in London — Kasparov scored zero wins against the Berlin in 15 games, the most stunning opening upset of the modern era. The Berlin has been Black's premier drawing weapon ever since. When Caruana needs a half-point as Black against 1.e4, he plays the Berlin — and at the elite level, the Berlin Endgame is essentially unloseable for Black with accurate play. His preparation in the technical positions arising after 9.Qxd8+ Kxd8 is among the deepest in the world. If you're trying to win against Caruana's Berlin, you'd better know more theory than he does — and that's almost impossible.
keyIdeas: After 4.O-O Nxe4, the queen trade is forced. Black plays 5.d4 Nd6 6.Bxc6 dxc6 7.dxe5 Nf5 8.Qxd8+ Kxd8 — this is the Berlin Endgame. Memorise this sequence; one wrong move is a lost game.
The king on e8 (then d8, then f7) is FINE. Looks exposed but White's attacking pieces are gone — no queens, soon to trade rooks. The king becomes an active piece in the endgame.
Both bishops on c8 and f8 are your trumps. Bishop pair in open queenless positions is worth 0.5 pawns — compensation for the doubled c-pawns. Don't trade lightly.
The …c5 break is the endgame's strategic theme. After …Bd7 + …Re8 + …Ke8-f7, eventually push …c5 opening the position. The doubled c-pawns become irrelevant when one of them is queening.
traps: If White plays aggressively in the endgame (e.g., trying to use the e5-pawn for kingside attack), Black exploits the overextension with precise defence: …Bd7 + …Bc5 + …Re8 piling up on the e5-pawn. Most Berlin White-side attacks are punished by simple development.

variations.name: Berlin Endgame Main Line
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 Nd6 Bxc6 dxc6 dxe5 Nf5 Qxd8+ Kxd8 Nc3 Ke8 h3 Be7 Bf4 Be6 Rad1 h6
variations.explanation: The Berlin Endgame in its purest form. After the forced trade sequence 5.d4 Nd6 6.Bxc6 dxc6 7.dxe5 Nf5 8.Qxd8+ Kxd8, the queens are off the board. Caruana continues 9.Nc3 Ke8 (the king starts walking back to safety) 10.h3 Be7 11.Bf4 Be6 12.Rad1 h6 — full development with the king tucked away. The position is theoretically equal but practically Black has more chances thanks to the bishop pair AND the technical complexity that favours the better-prepared side.

variations.name: Berlin 5.Re1 Sideline
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 Re1 Nd6 Nxe5 Be7 Bf1 Nxe5 Rxe5 O-O d4 Bf6 Re1 Re8 c3 Rxe1 Qxe1 Ne8
variations.explanation: When White avoids the queen trade with 5.Re1 (instead of 5.d4), Black equalises with calm development. After 5...Nd6 6.Nxe5 Be7 7.Bf1 (the bishop retreats from the Bb5-pin) Nxe5 8.Rxe5 O-O 9.d4 Bf6 — Black has completed development without the queen trade. After further exchanges 10.Re1 Re8 11.c3 Rxe1 12.Qxe1 Ne8 — the position is balanced, Caruana plays for the endgame technique.

variations.name: Anti-Berlin 4.d3
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 Nf6 d3 Bc5 Bxc6 dxc6 Nbd2 Be6 O-O Bd6 Nb3 Nd7
variations.explanation: When White plays 4.d3 (the Anti-Berlin to avoid the queen trade), Caruana plays the active 4...Bc5 development. After 5.Bxc6 dxc6 (the standard doubled-c-pawn structure) 6.Nbd2 Be6 7.O-O Bd6 (rerouting the bishop) 8.Nb3 Nd7 — Black keeps the bishop pair AND prepares the …f6 break + …O-O. Long-side castling is also an option. The position is balanced; Caruana plays for the middlegame chances his preparation gives him.

openings.name: Italian Game (Firouzja)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 cxd4 Bb4+ Nc3 Nxe4 O-O Bxc3 bxc3 d5 Ba3 dxc4 Re1 Be6 Rxe4
openings.overview: Firouzja's Italian is what happens when the youngest player to reach 2800 FIDE (he hit it in 2021 at age 18 — breaking Carlsen's record) decides 'I want a chaos opening, but with sound positional foundations.' The Italian Game itself is among chess's oldest — Greco's 1620 manuscripts are filled with Italian Game analyses, and Paul Morphy's 1858 European tour included devastating Italian attacks. The Evans Gambit (a sharp Italian sub-variation) was invented by Welsh sea captain William Davies Evans in 1827 and championed by Anderssen, Morphy, and most famously Kasparov who revived it against Anand at the 1995 Riga tournament. Where Caruana plays the IQP for technique, Firouzja plays it for the bishop pair gambit — sacrificing a pawn to reach positions with two raking bishops and open lines. Risk-tolerant, unforgiving, fast.
keyIdeas: The c3+d4 break PLUS the Nc3 sacrifice. Firouzja's line goes 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 Bb4+ 7.Nc3! (not Bd2) — accepting the …Nxe4 grab on purpose, because the resulting open position with the bishop pair compensates the pawn.
Ba3 is the key attacking move. After …Bxc3 bxc3 d5 Ba3! — the bishop fires down the a3-f8 diagonal at Black's king-side, AND prevents Black from castling safely.
Use the open e-file and the bishop pair together. The Re1 lift + Bc4 (or Bb3) + Ba3 creates a three-piece attack on Black's central files.
The Evans Gambit lives in Firouzja's repertoire as a surprise weapon. When opponents have prepared the Italian, the Evans (with 4.b4!) takes them out of book entirely.
traps: After 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 Bb4+ 7.Nc3 Nxe4 8.O-O Bxc3 9.bxc3 d5 10.Ba3! — Black's king is permanently stuck on e8 because Ba3 prevents castling kingside. After …dxc4 11.Re1 Be6 12.Rxe4! — exchange sacrifice for further attacking play. Pure Firouzja tactical violence.
warnings: DON'T play the slow Italian (d3 system) if you want Firouzja's gambit chances. The d4 break is necessary; without it, you're playing a passive Italian that doesn't fit Firouzja's style.

Watch for Black's …d5 + …Be6 counterplay. If Black challenges the centre AND develops the bishop accurately, your initiative can fizzle. Maintain pressure with concrete moves, not passive development.

variations.name: Sharp Gambit Line
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 cxd4 Bb4+ Nc3 Nxe4 O-O Bxc3 bxc3 d5 Ba3 dxc4 Re1 Be6
variations.explanation: Firouzja's flagship Italian gambit line. After 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 Bb4+ — instead of the safe 7.Bd2, Firouzja plays 7.Nc3! offering the e4-pawn. Black accepts with 7...Nxe4 8.O-O Bxc3 9.bxc3 — White now has doubled c-pawns but a beautiful initiative. 9...d5 (Black tries to defend the e-file) 10.Ba3! (preventing kingside castling AND eyeing f8) dxc4 11.Re1 Be6 — White has the bishop pair, the open e-file, the a3-bishop pinning, and Black still hasn't castled. The compensation is more than the pawn.

variations.name: Giuoco Pianissimo
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 d3 Nf6 O-O d6 c3 a6 a4 Ba7 Nbd2 O-O Re1 Be6 Bb3 h6
variations.explanation: When Firouzja wants a slower game (rare, but sometimes), he plays the d3 system. After standard development through 11.Re1 Be6 12.Bb3 h6 — both sides reach a typical Italian middlegame with the centre closed. The plan: Nf1-Ng3 + h3 + Nh4 + f4 for a kingside attack. Same Italian, slower tempo. Not Firouzja's preferred style, but he handles it accurately when needed.

variations.name: Evans Gambit Spirit
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 b4 Bxb4 c3 Ba5 d4 exd4 O-O d6 cxd4 Bb6 Nc3 Bg4
variations.explanation: The Evans Gambit — Firouzja's chaos weapon when opponents have prepared mainline Italian. 4.b4! sacrifices a pawn for tempo (4...Bxb4 5.c3 Ba5) and central control (6.d4 exd4 7.O-O d6 8.cxd4). After 8...Bb6 9.Nc3 Bg4 — Black tries to pin the f3-knight. White's plan: Bb2 + Qb3 + Nd5 piling up on the kingside while Black's queen and king are still on the queenside. The Evans is theoretically unsound but practically lethal at every level except top-10 GMs.

trapLines.name: Nxe4 Grab Punished by d5
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 Nxe4
trapLines.explanation: When Black greedily grabs e4 with the knight (instead of the principled 5...exd4), White plays 6.d5! immediately. The d5-pawn forks the c6-knight (which has to move) AND attacks Black's bishop on c5 indirectly. After 6...Ne7 (the only square — Nb8 wastes more tempo) 7.dxc6 — White wins material AND keeps the central pawn duo. Classic Italian punishment for amateurs who 'just grab the pawn'.

openings.name: Sicilian Rossolimo (Firouzja)
openings.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 g6 Bxc6 dxc6 d3 Bg7 h3 Nf6 Nc3 Nd7 Be3 e5 Qd2 O-O O-O-O b5
openings.overview: Firouzja's Rossolimo is his anti-theory weapon against the Sicilian. The system bears the name of Nicolas Rossolimo, a Russian-American GM who fled the Russian Revolution as a child and became a celebrated New York chess teacher in the 1950s-60s — he systematized the 3.Bb5 approach and scored exceptional results with it across his career. The Rossolimo gained elite respectability when Magnus Carlsen began championing it in the 2010s as a way to dodge the Najdorf without sacrificing winning chances. By playing 3.Bb5 you wipe the slate clean: no Najdorf, no Dragon, no Sveshnikov. Black has to think from move 3. Firouzja's preference is the immediate Bxc6 commitment — sacrifice the bishop pair for a permanent structural advantage. The doubled c-pawns become a long-term target that he attacks throughout the middlegame with characteristic aggression.
keyIdeas: Bb5 + Bxc6 is one decision. Most Rossolimo players hold the pin for tempo gain; Firouzja trades immediately, accepting the bishop pair concession for the permanent structural damage.
After …dxc6 (the standard recapture), Black has doubled c-pawns AND the c-file isn't fully open. Target c6 and c7 with rook lifts and knight outposts for the entire middlegame.
O-O-O queenside castling is Firouzja's signature plan. Against opposite-side-castled Black, the g4-g5 pawn storm arrives faster than Black's queenside counterplay.
The kingside attack uses pieces, not just pawns. The Bf4 + Qd2 + Bh6 reroute is the standard plan — trade off Black's fianchetto bishop, then attack the dark squares with everything.
traps: If Black plays 3…g6 (fianchetto) and you respond 4.Bxc6 bxc6 (the WORSE recapture for Black), Black's pawn structure has isolated a/c pawns. After 5.O-O Bg7 6.Re1 — the open b-file and weak a-pawn give White a positional win.
warnings: Don't trade pieces unnecessarily. The doubled c-pawn structural advantage matters MOST when there are still pieces on the board to attack with. In simplified endgames, Black often holds.

Respect Black's bishop pair. Even though you've damaged their structure, the two bishops in open positions can create dynamic compensation if Black plays actively. Keep the position closed when possible.

variations.name: 3...g6 Main Line
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 g6 Bxc6 dxc6 d3 Bg7 h3 Nf6 Nc3 Nd7 Be3 e5 Qd2 O-O O-O-O b5
variations.explanation: Firouzja's flagship Rossolimo setup. After 3...g6 (Black's most popular reply) 4.Bxc6! dxc6 (the principled recapture — keeps the c-pawn structure) 5.d3 (modest, supports future e4 stability) Bg7 6.h3 (preventing …Bg4 pin) Nf6 7.Nc3 Nd7 (Black reroutes knight from f6 to defend e5) 8.Be3 e5 (Black locks the centre) 9.Qd2 O-O 10.O-O-O — White castles long, signalling the kingside attack. Black's 10...b5 starts counterplay on the queenside but White's attack arrives first thanks to the natural g4-g5 push.

variations.name: 3...e6 Variation
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 e6 Bxc6 bxc6 d3 Ne7 O-O Ng6 e5
variations.explanation: When Black plays 3...e6 instead of the fianchetto, the recapture 4.Bxc6 bxc6 (forced — …dxc6 here misplaces the d-pawn) creates a different doubled-pawn structure. White plays 5.d3 (calm) Ne7 (knight reroute via …Ng6) 6.O-O Ng6 7.e5! — the central push cramps Black's pieces. The Ne7-g6 manoeuvre is the only useful Black plan; White's structural and space edge favours the long-term grind.

openings.name: Vienna Game (Firouzja)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nc3 Nf6 Bc4 Bc5 d3 d6 f4 Nc6 Nf3 Bg4 Na4 Bb6 Nxb6 axb6
openings.overview: Firouzja's Vienna Game is the King's Gambit's smarter cousin. Named after the Viennese chess circles of the 1860s-1880s where Wilhelm Steinitz (the first official World Champion) and his students experimented with it, the Vienna was a popular tournament weapon through the early 1900s before the Italian and Spanish overshadowed it. Modern engine preparation has resurrected it: Vassily Ivanchuk and Adams use it occasionally at elite level. Firouzja's aggressive style fits perfectly — by tucking Nc3 in first, you defend e4 and keep the f-pawn free for either f4 (gambit) or f3 (solid). Either way, opponents who autopilot through 2.Nc3 see f4 on move 3 and immediately have problems they haven't studied.
keyIdeas: Nc3 BEFORE Nf3 is the entire opening's reason for existing. The reversed order protects e4 (so 3.f4 doesn't hang the pawn after …Nxe4) AND keeps Nf3 in reserve for after the f-pawn moves.
Against 2…Nf6 (most common), 3.f4 is the principled Vienna Gambit. After 3...d5 (Black's only critical reply) 4.fxe5 Nxe4, you've sacrificed a pawn for open lines and active piece development.
Against 2…Nc6 (Italian-like setup), play 3.Bc4 first (or 3.f4 for the violent version). The Nc3+Bc4 combo aims at f7 with the standard Italian-style pressure plus the Vienna's central control.

The Vienna transitions to King's Gambit-style attacks but with one improvement: your knight is on c3, NOT b1. That extra piece in the attack is the difference between a sound gambit and an unsound one.
traps: After 2…Nf6 3.Bc4 Nxe4?? (the natural-looking pawn grab) — 4.Qh5! creates double threats: Qxf7# AND Qxe5+ winning the knight. Black's only saving move is 4...Nd6 (defending mate but losing material). Pure Vienna shot.
warnings: Don't play 3.f4 if Black hasn't committed pieces yet. After move-orders like 2.Nc3 Bc5 (skipping …Nf6), the f4 push hangs the e-pawn AND weakens your king prematurely.
Watch for …d5 equalising. In ANY Vienna position, if Black gets in …d5 before you've completed development, the centre dissolves and your opening edge vanishes. Prevent or punish …d5 specifically.

variations.name: Vienna with f4
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nc3 Nf6 Bc4 Bc5 d3 d6 f4 Nc6 Nf3 Bg4 Na4 Bb6 Nxb6 axb6
variations.explanation: Firouzja's positional Vienna setup. After 2...Nf6 3.Bc4 Bc5 4.d3 (calm development) d6 5.f4 (the attacking signal) Nc6 6.Nf3 Bg4 (Black pins) 7.Na4! (sidestepping the pin AND attacking the c5-bishop) Bb6 8.Nxb6 axb6 — Black's pawn structure is now doubled on b6+b7. The Na4-Nxb6 manoeuvre is the line's signature: trade off Black's most active piece, accept the structural concession for the long-term positional edge.

variations.name: Vienna Gambit
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nc3 Nf6 f4 d5 fxe5 Nxe4 Nf3 Bc5 d4 Bb4 Bd3 Nxc3 bxc3 Bxc3+ Bd2 Bxa1 Qxa1
variations.explanation: The Vienna Gambit at its most aggressive — a classic example of investing material for piece activity. After 3.f4 d5! (the principled reply) 4.fxe5 Nxe4 5.Nf3 Bc5 6.d4 Bb4 (Black pins the knight) 7.Bd3 Nxc3 8.bxc3 Bxc3+ 9.Bd2 Bxa1 10.Qxa1 — White is down an exchange (rook for bishop) but has the bishop pair, the open f-file, the central pawn duo e5+d4, and Black hasn't castled. The compensation is real; the position is genuinely murky for both sides. Pure attacking chess.

openings.name: Sicilian Najdorf (Firouzja)
openings.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be3 e5 Nb3 Be7 f3 Be6 Qd2 Nbd7 O-O-O b5 g4 Nb6
openings.overview: Firouzja plays the Najdorf with the fearlessness of youth — and the preparation of an engine. The opening is named after Argentine GM Miguel Najdorf, who fled Poland for Argentina in 1939 right before WWII (his entire family was killed in the Holocaust); he made his new country his home and pioneered the …a6 move in the 1940s. Bobby Fischer immortalised it in his 1972 World Championship run, Kasparov played it for thirty years against Karpov, and Firouzja represents the next generation — the youngest player to reach 2800 FIDE (2021, age 18) brings 21st-century engine preparation to one of chess's oldest theoretical battlegrounds. His Najdorf is characterized by deep preparation in the English Attack opposite-side castling races.
keyIdeas: …a6 (the Najdorf move) is prophylaxis disguised as development — it prevents White's Nb5 ideas (which would fork c7 and d6) AND prepares the …b5 queenside expansion. One move, two strategic functions.

Develop with Be7, Be6, Nbd7 for maximum flexibility. Don't commit the king until you've seen White's setup — castling kingside into White's pawn storm in the English Attack is the most common Najdorf mistake.
Counter on the queenside with …b5 while defending the kingside. The …b5–b4 push forces White's Nc3 to move, opening lines for Black's attack on the white king (if White castled long).

The Najdorf rewards calculation. Every move can be a tactical resource: Nd5 sacrifices in many lines, …b4 pawn pushes, exchange sacrifices on c3. Firouzja's edge is finding the right move 5 seconds faster than opponents.
traps: In the English Attack after 6.Be3 e5 7.Nb3 Be7, if White plays 8.g4 too aggressively (before completing kingside development), Black's 8...b5 starts a fierce counterattack — the queenside files open faster than White's g-file attack arrives.
warnings: The Najdorf requires PRECISE knowledge. Without engine-checked preparation in your specific variation, you can be checkmated in 25 moves by a player who knows the line cold. Don't play 'on principle' in the Najdorf.

Nd5 sacrifices are everywhere. In Be3/Bg5/Be2 systems, the Nd5 sac is the position's defining tactical theme. Always calculate it — even if it looks impossible — before making any move that allows it.

variations.name: English Attack
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be3 e5 Nb3 Be7 f3 Be6 Qd2 Nbd7 O-O-O b5 g4 Nb6
variations.explanation: The sharpest Najdorf line — the English Attack with opposite-side castling. After 6.Be3 e5 7.Nb3 Be7 (Black's flexible bishop development) 8.f3 (preparing g4) Be6 (controlling d5) 9.Qd2 Nbd7 10.O-O-O — White's king is on c1, the race is on. Black plays 10...b5! (queenside attack starts immediately) 11.g4 Nb6 (the knight reroutes to support …b4) — both sides are aimed at the opposing king. The winner is the side who calculates faster; in Firouzja's case, that's almost always him.

variations.name: 6.Bg5 Main Line
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Bg5 e6 f4 Be7 Qf3 Qc7 O-O-O Nbd7 Bd3 b5 Rhe1 Bb7
variations.explanation: Against 6.Bg5 (the classical attacking system), Firouzja plays the standard …e6 setup (not the Poisoned Pawn 6...Qb6!?). After 7.f4 Be7 8.Qf3 Qc7 9.O-O-O Nbd7 10.Bd3 b5 11.Rhe1 Bb7 — both sides have completed development for the opposite-side race. Black's …Bb7 fianchetto aims at the white king on c1; the …b5–b4 push is coming. Firouzja's tactical sense in these razor-sharp positions ranks among the world's best.

variations.name: 6.Be2 Classical
variations.pgn: e4 c5 Nf3 d6 d4 cxd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 a6 Be2 e5 Nb3 Be7 O-O O-O Be3 Be6 Qd2 Nbd7 a4 Rc8 f3 Qc7
variations.explanation: Against the calm 6.Be2 system (Karpov's preference and now Magnus's main weapon), Firouzja plays the textbook setup: …e5 forcing Nb3, then …Be7/…O-O for king safety, …Be6/…Nbd7 controlling d5, …Rc8/…Qc7 for queenside pressure. A more strategic game than the English Attack but Firouzja navigates it well — the position is balanced with active piece play for both sides.

openings.name: Grunfeld Defense (Firouzja)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Rb1 O-O Be2 cxd4 cxd4 Qa5+ Bd2 Qxa2 O-O Bg4
openings.overview: Firouzja uses the Grünfeld to fight for wins as Black against 1.d4. Austrian master Ernst Grünfeld invented this defence in 1922 against Aron Nimzowitsch — letting White have the centre, then attacking it from the wings. Fischer played it as his main weapon in his 1972 World Championship match; Kasparov-Karpov 1986-1990 featured legendary Grünfeld battles where Kasparov's preparation drove the theory deeper than anyone imagined; Carlsen made it his wins-with-Black weapon. Firouzja brings the engine-era preparation to the famously sharp Exchange Variation — including the modern …Qa5+/…Qxa2 pawn-grab idea that requires nerves of steel.
keyIdeas: The Grünfeld philosophy: LET White build d4+e4+c3, then dismantle it with …c5 + …Bg7 pressure + …Qa5/Qb6 tactics. Hypermodern strategy in pure form.
The Bg7 is your most important piece — it stares at d4 (and via the long diagonal, at a1 where White's rook lives) for the entire game. Never trade lightly.
The …Qa5+ → …Qxa2 pawn grab is a real material grab, NOT a tactical blunder. In the Exchange Variation, White's c-pawn structure is doubled and the a2-pawn is structurally weak. Take it.

Calculate sharper than your opponent. The Grünfeld rewards concrete calculation in 1990s-style theoretical positions — Firouzja's edge is engine-era preparation in lines older players still play from memory.
traps: After 7.Bg7 8.Nf3 c5 9.Rb1 (the modern Rb1 setup), if White plays 10.Be3 (instead of the principled 10.Be2), Black has 10...Qa5+! 11.Bd2 Qxa2 — grabbing the a-pawn cleanly because the b1-rook can't easily defend it. Pure positional theft.
warnings: Don't let White consolidate the centre. Every move you spend NOT challenging d4+e4 is a move White gets to build a stable structure that becomes unbreakable.

The a2-pawn grab is dangerous if you let White's initiative compound. After grabbing the pawn, you MUST complete development quickly (…Bg4, …Nc6, …Rfd8) — if White's pieces all reach attacking squares first, your queen on a2 becomes a target.

variations.name: Exchange Variation with Qa5+
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Rb1 O-O Be2 cxd4 cxd4 Qa5+ Bd2 Qxa2 O-O Bg4
variations.explanation: Firouzja's most ambitious Grünfeld line — the queen-pawn grab. After the standard Exchange Variation 7.Bg7 8.Nf3 c5 9.Rb1 (the modern setup) O-O 10.Be2 cxd4 11.cxd4 — Black plays 11...Qa5+! 12.Bd2 Qxa2! grabbing the a-pawn cleanly. Now 13.O-O Bg4 — Black is up a pawn AND has the bishop pin on f3. White has central pieces but no concrete attack. Black completes development with …Nc6, …Rac8, …Rfd8, and the extra pawn wins the long game. Engine-era Grünfeld: take the pawn, defend, win.

variations.name: Russian System with Qb3
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Bg7 Qb3 dxc4 Qxc4 O-O e4 a6 Be2 b5 Qb3 c5 dxc5 Be6 Qc2 Nbd7
variations.explanation: The Russian System with 5.Qb3 was Soviet GM Smyslov's preferred anti-Grünfeld; the queen defends c4 from b3. Firouzja's response: 5...dxc4 6.Qxc4 O-O 7.e4 a6 8.Be2 b5 (Black grabs queenside space) 9.Qb3 c5 10.dxc5 Be6 11.Qc2 Nbd7 — Black has aggressive queenside expansion AND central pressure. The …b5 + …c5 + …Be6 combo aims at White's centre while developing minor pieces.

variations.name: Fianchetto Grunfeld
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Be2 O-O O-O Nc6 Be3 cxd4 cxd4 Bg4 d5 Na5 Nd4
variations.explanation: When White plays the modest Be2 setup (instead of the aggressive Bc4 lines), Black faces a slower structural battle. After 10.Be2 O-O 11.O-O Nc6 (developing) 12.Be3 cxd4 13.cxd4 (the standard pawn capture sequence) Bg4 14.d5 (White pushes the centre) Na5 — Black's knight reaches a queenside outpost attacking c4. The 15.Nd4 reroute brings White's knight to a central square. Strategic Grünfeld where both sides manoeuvre for the better piece placement.

openings.name: French Defense (Firouzja)
openings.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 dxe4 Nxe4 Be7 Bxf6 gxf6 Nf3 b6 Bc4 Bb7 Qe2 Nd7 O-O-O
openings.overview: Firouzja's French Defence is a surprise weapon when he wants to drag 1.e4 players out of their comfort zone. The French was famously played in the 1834 La Bourdonnais–McDonnell match (the first 'world championship' match of the modern era), making it one of chess's oldest defensive systems. It was Aaron Nimzowitsch's main weapon (he played it for 30 years and considered it 'theoretically inferior but practically excellent'); Mikhail Botvinnik used it to win World Championship games; Wolfgang Uhlmann championed it at elite level for decades. The Burn Variation (4...dxe4, named for English master Amos Burn in the late 1800s) is Firouzja's choice — the doubled f-pawns look ugly but give Black excellent central control and unbalanced positions where calculation decides.
keyIdeas: …e6 + …d5 builds the French pawn chain — the structural backbone of every French variation. The downside: the c8-bishop is initially locked behind the e-pawn; the upside: the structure is extraordinarily solid.
In the Burn Variation (4...dxe4 5.Nxe4 Be7 6.Bxf6 gxf6), Black accepts doubled f-pawns for two reasons: the bishop pair AND the half-open g-file for the rook. Looks worse than it plays.

The light-squared bishop (the famous 'French bishop') is the opening's eternal problem. Solutions: …b6 + …Bb7 (long-diagonal fianchetto), …Bd7 → …Be8 → …Bg6 (slow reroute), or trading via …Bxg5 in early lines.
Aim for opposite-side castling chaos. In the Burn Variation, after the bishop trade on f6, Black often castles queenside — the king on c8 is safe enough and the kingside files open for attack.
traps: In the Winawer with 3...Bb4 (a different French line), if White plays 4.a3 and Black retreats 4...Ba5, the bishop can later swing to c7 to defend the kingside — but in the modern lines, 4...Bxc3+ trading is usually correct.
warnings: The light-squared bishop is the French player's lifelong project. Plan its development from move 3 — without a clear path for the c8-bishop, you reach move 20 with a piece that has only 2 useful squares.
In the Advance Variation (3.e5), the …c5 break must come immediately. If you delay …c5 for even one move, White consolidates the centre and the French becomes a positionally lost defence.

variations.name: Burn Variation 4...dxe4
variations.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 dxe4 Nxe4 Be7 Bxf6 gxf6 Nf3 b6 Bc4 Bb7 Qe2 Nd7 O-O-O
variations.explanation: Firouzja's preferred French line — the Burn Variation. After 4.Nc3 Nf6 (Black develops naturally) 5.Bg5 (White's classical pin) dxe4! (Black's principled gambit — sacrificing the pawn for piece play) 6.Nxe4 Be7 7.Bxf6 (White must trade) gxf6 — yes, doubled f-pawns, but Black has the bishop pair AND will get the open g-file for the rook. 8.Nf3 b6 (fianchetto preparation) 9.Bc4 Bb7 10.Qe2 Nd7 11.O-O-O — White castles queenside and Black follows suit, reaching an unbalanced middlegame where Firouzja's tactical sense flourishes. The 'ugly' pawn structure is actually fine.

variations.name: Classical 4...Be7
variations.pgn: e4 e6 d4 d5 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e5 Nfd7 Bxe7 Qxe7 f4 O-O Nf3 c5 Qd2 Nc6 dxc5 Nxc5 O-O-O f6
variations.explanation: When Firouzja wants a more traditional French, he plays 4...Be7 (instead of the Burn's 4...dxe4). After 5.e5 Nfd7 (the standard knight retreat) 6.Bxe7 Qxe7 7.f4 (White's standard expansion) O-O 8.Nf3 c5 (the equalising break) 9.Qd2 Nc6 10.dxc5 Nxc5 11.O-O-O f6 — Black has reached a typical French structure with the …c5 break completed. The position is balanced with chances on both wings.

openings.name: Ruy Lopez (Firouzja)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 d6 c3 O-O h3 Bb7 d4 Re8 Nbd2 Bf8 a4 h6 Bc2 exd4 cxd4 Nb4
openings.overview: Firouzja plays the Ruy Lopez with the bold attacking style of a teenager who just broke into the world's top 5. The Ruy Lopez (named after Spanish priest Ruy López de Segura who analyzed it in his 1561 book) is chess's most studied opening — every World Champion from Steinitz to Carlsen has lived and died by its theory. The Chigorin Variation honours Russian master Mikhail Chigorin (late 1800s); the Zaitsev Variation was developed by Soviet trainer Igor Zaitsev (Karpov's longtime second). Firouzja brings 21st-century engine preparation to lines that have been analysed for 460 years — finding novelties where everyone thought the theory was finished.
keyIdeas: Build the classic d4 centre slowly: c3 + h3 + Re1 + d4 in that order. Patience matters — premature d4 lets Black equalise with central exchanges.

The Bb3 → Bc2 manoeuvre keeps the bishop on the b1-h7 diagonal aimed at the kingside. After …d5 by Black, the bishop is redirected to attack from c2; without this reroute, the bishop becomes a tall pawn.
a4 is the queenside probe. By poking Black's b-pawn with a4, you force …b4 (closing the queenside) or …bxa4 (giving up structure). Either way, you've gained information AND tempo.

Watch for the Marshall Attack. If Black plays …d5 instead of …d6 (the gambit), you face the most prepared opening in chess. Be ready with the Anti-Marshall (a4) OR have your Marshall defensive lines memorised.
traps: After 5.O-O Be7 6.Re1 b5 7.Bb3 d6 8.c3 — if Black plays the natural 8...Na5? too early (without first preparing with …O-O), 9.Bc2 d5 10.exd5 e4 11.Nxe5 — White wins material thanks to the loose Na5 and Black's underdeveloped pieces.
warnings: Ruy Lopez requires PATIENCE. Most amateurs lose Ruy positions by trying to force tactical breakthroughs before the position is ready. Build slowly, exchange wisely, win the technical phase.
Watch for the Marshall Attack: 8.c3 d5! (instead of the safe 8...d6). If you haven't prepared the Anti-Marshall lines, ALWAYS play 8.h3 first (the Anti-Marshall move-order) to prevent the gambit.

variations.name: Chigorin Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 d6 c3 O-O h3 Na5 Bc2 c5 d4 Qc7 Nbd2 Nc6 d5 Nd8 Nf1
variations.explanation: The Chigorin Variation (named after Mikhail Chigorin, late 1800s) — one of the most strategically rich Ruy Lopez systems. The trademark knight tour Nf6-Na5-Nc6-Nd8 (yes, all the way back!) prepares …Ne6 supporting the kingside. After 13.d5 (White closes the centre) Nd8 14.Nf1 (knight reroutes to g3) — both sides finish development. The middlegame plan: White attacks the kingside with f4-f5; Black defends with …g6 + …Nh7-Ng5. Slow positional chess at its richest.

variations.name: Zaitsev Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 d6 c3 O-O h3 Bb7 d4 Re8 Nbd2 Bf8 a4 h6 Bc2 exd4 cxd4 Nb4
variations.explanation: The Zaitsev (developed by Karpov's second Igor Zaitsev in the 1970s-80s) — used extensively in the Karpov-Kasparov matches. Black plays …Bb7 (fianchetto) + …Re8 (eyeing the e-file) for central counterplay. After 14.a4 (the queenside probe) h6 (preparing …Nh7) 15.Bc2 exd4 16.cxd4 Nb4 — Black attacks the c2-bishop, gaining tempo. The Zaitsev features sharp tactical play in lines that look quiet.

variations.name: Open Ruy Lopez
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Nxe4 d4 b5 Bb3 d5 dxe5 Be6 Nbd2 Nc5 c3 Be7 Bc2 Bg4 Re1 O-O
variations.explanation: The Open Ruy Lopez — when Black grabs the e4-pawn with 5...Nxe4 instead of playing solidly. After 6.d4 b5 7.Bb3 d5 8.dxe5 Be6 — Black has temporarily won a pawn but White has the strong e5-cramping piece. 9.Nbd2 Nc5 10.c3 Be7 11.Bc2 Bg4 12.Re1 O-O — Black has activated all pieces; White has central control. The position is sharp and unbalanced, perfect for Firouzja's tactical style.

openings.name: King's Indian Defense (Firouzja)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 f3 f5 Be3 f4 Bf2 g5
openings.overview: Firouzja plays the King's Indian with the violence of youth — and the preparation of an engine. The KID has a 100-year pedigree as Black's most violent answer to 1.d4: pioneered by the hypermoderns (Réti, Nimzowitsch) in the 1920s, perfected by David Bronstein and Mikhail Tal in the 1950s for spectacular attacking play, and immortalised by Bobby Fischer (1972 World Championship match) and Garry Kasparov who used it for a decade against Karpov. The Mar del Plata variation is named for the 1953 Mar del Plata tournament in Argentina where Pal Benko introduced the modern attacking treatment. Firouzja brings this 100-year-old opening into the engine era with novelties in lines other players think are exhausted.
keyIdeas: The Bg7 fianchetto is non-negotiable. The bishop on g7 controls the long a1-h8 diagonal forever, supports kingside attacks AND aims at White's queenside. Never trade it lightly.
Once the centre locks with d5, the game splits in two: White attacks queenside (c5 push), Black attacks kingside (f5-f4-g5). The faster attack wins, and Black's attack is usually faster.
The knight tour Nf6-h5-f4 (or via Nf6-e8-g7-f5-f4) lands the knight on f4 attacking d3, e2, g2, h3 simultaneously. This is the KID's single most violent piece-tour.
Don't fear the closed centre. A locked centre means no White counterplay; you're guaranteed time to mobilise the kingside attack. The d4-d5 closure is the KID's friend, not enemy.
traps: After 7.d5 Ne7, if White plays passively (no g3, no h3, no kingside defensive prep), Black launches the immediate f5-f4-g5-g4 pawn cascade — the kingside attack arrives in 8-10 moves and is often unstoppable.
warnings: Watch for White's c5-c6 queenside breakthrough. If you ignore the queenside and White gets c5 → c6 → cxd6 cxd6 → Nc6 outpost, your queenside collapses faster than your kingside attack matures.
DON'T play …f5 if White can respond exf5 opening the centre. The centre must lock with d5 first; an open centre makes …f5 a structural liability.

variations.name: Mar del Plata Main Line
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 f3 f5 Be3 f4 Bf2 g5
variations.explanation: The Mar del Plata variation — named for the 1953 Mar del Plata tournament. The sharpest King's Indian position in chess theory: after 8.d5 Ne7 9.Ne1 (White reroutes the knight) Nd7 10.f3 f5! 11.Be3 f4! (the locking move — kingside is now permanent attack territory) 12.Bf2 g5! — Black's kingside pawn cascade is launched. White's only defensive idea is the slow Nd3-Nf2-Nf1 reroute supporting g3 break; meanwhile Black's …Ng6 + …Rf6-Rh6 lift creates unstoppable mating threats. Firouzja's tactical depth in these positions wins decisive games at elite level.

variations.name: Classical 9.Nd2
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Nd2 a5 a3 Nd7 Rb1 f5 b4 Nf6 f3 f4
variations.explanation: When White plays 9.Nd2 (instead of the Mar del Plata's 9.Ne1), preparing a slower queenside attack with b4, Firouzja plays 9...a5! immediately — preventing the b4 push and gaining queenside space. After 10.a3 Nd7 11.Rb1 f5 12.b4 Nf6 13.f3 f4 — Black gets the same kingside attack as the Mar del Plata but with the queenside locked (so White has less counterplay). Strategically a better KID line for Black.

variations.name: Bayonet Defense
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 b4 Nh5 Re1 f5 Ng5 Nf6 Bf3 c6 dxc6 Nxc6
variations.explanation: Against the Bayonet Attack (Kramnik's 9.b4 anti-KID weapon — White grabs queenside space before Black can lock the centre), Firouzja plays 9...Nh5! immediately — the knight prepares …f5 with one tempo. After 10.Re1 f5 11.Ng5 Nf6 (forcing the trade of attacking knights) 12.Bf3 c6 (Black challenges the centre) 13.dxc6 Nxc6 — Black has opened the position WITHIN the KID structure, giving Firouzja the dynamic chances his calculation thrives on.

openings.name: The Loud Italian / Dubov Gambit
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 b4 Bb6 e5 Ne4 Bd5 Nxc3 Nxc3 dxc3 Bg5 Ne7 O-O
openings.overview: Dubov's signature opening — a delayed Evans Gambit in the Italian Game introduced via his immortal win over Karjakin at the Russian Championship Superfinal 2020. The move 6.b4!? (after the standard Italian Giuoco Piano move order) sacrifices a pawn for explosive piece play, and the position becomes so complex that even strong engines struggle to evaluate it. Dubov's Chessable course 'Dubov's Explosive Italian' walks through the gambit and the romantic attacking ideas it unleashes. This is old-school chess for the 21st century: trade material for activity, attack the king, and trust your calculation. Source: Dubov-Karjakin, Russian Championship 2020 R11.
keyIdeas: Play c3 + d4 in the Giuoco Piano first to lull Black into a normal Italian, THEN drop the b4 bomb on move 6 — the surprise factor is half the gambit's power
After 6.b4 Bb6, 7.e5 chases the f6-knight while gaining a huge centre — the central pawn wedge becomes the engine of the attack
The d-pawn that Black wins is a long-term liability sitting on d4/c3 — White's pieces use the open lines to whip up a kingside attack while Black tries to consolidate the gambit pawn
Look for sacrificial queen ideas (Qxg6, Bxg6, Bxf7+) once Black's pieces are tied to defending the king — Dubov's immortal game ended with a Queen sacrifice followed by a forced mating net
traps: If Black declines the gambit and tries to develop normally with ...d6, White still gets a huge centre with e5 + d-pawn pressure
Watch for ...d5 counter-thrusts — Black's only real way to disrupt the attack is a timely central break
warnings: This is a SHARP gambit — only play it if you're ready to calculate concrete tactical lines deeply. Engines DO criticise it objectively
Don't play it in classical time controls against well-prepared opponents — they'll find the safe routes. It thrives in rapid/blitz and against the unprepared

variations.name: Main Line (Dubov vs Karjakin 2020)
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 b4 Bb6 e5 Ne4 Bd5 Nxc3 Nxc3 dxc3 Bg5 Ne7 O-O
variations.explanation: The exact opening from Dubov's immortal win over Karjakin. After 6.b4 Bb6 7.e5, the f6-knight has no safe square except e4 (where it gets traded). Bd5 attacks both knight and rook simultaneously; after the forced trade Black is up a pawn on c3 but White's piece activity is overwhelming. Dubov went on to sacrifice his queen at move 19 for a winning attack.

variations.name: Black Declines Gambit with ...Bb6
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 b4 Bb6 cxd4
variations.explanation: If Black retreats with 6...Bb6 and refuses to take on b4, White recaptures on d4 with the c-pawn, getting a big centre. Black's bishop on b6 is misplaced and Black has nothing to show for the loss of tempo.

variations.name: Bc5xb4 (Pure Evans Gambit transposition)
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bc4 Bc5 c3 Nf6 d4 exd4 b4 Bxb4 cxb4 Nxb4 Ba3 Nc6
variations.explanation: If Black accepts the pawn and re-takes on b4 with the bishop, White's c3 takes back. Ba3 immediately targets the king's-side dark squares — classic Evans Gambit themes with the bishop pair and central pressure.

openings.name: Catalan Opening (Dubov)
openings.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2 Be4 Qc1 Nc6
openings.overview: Dubov plays the Catalan like a creative mad scientist. The opening was christened at the 1929 Barcelona tournament when Savielly Tartakower fianchettoed his bishop and named it after the host region; Botvinnik and Kasparov used it occasionally before Vladimir Kramnik elevated it to championship status in the 2000s. Dubov's contribution: he found ways to make the slow positional Catalan into a SHARP tactical opening — sacrificing pawns and pieces to reach the dynamic positions his calculation rewards. While Carlsen and Caruana grind out wins from quiet Catalans, Dubov turns the same opening into a brawl. Source: Magnus Carlsen called Dubov 'the most creative player I've ever worked with' (Dubov was Carlsen's second for the 2018 World Championship match).
keyIdeas: The Bg2 is the Catalan's defining piece — it stares at the a8-rook from across the board AND supports queenside operations. Dubov often sacrifices the pawn structure to ACTIVATE the Bg2 sooner.
Recover the c4 pawn flexibly: Qc2 (mainline), Qa4+ (Karpov's idea), or a4 (positional probe). Each leads to different middlegame structures; Dubov picks based on what's most unbalancing.
Dubov's Gambit Catalan (8.Ne5!?) sacrifices the bishop pair on c6 for piece activity — engines call it equal, but practical results favour the side calculating concretely.
Look for dynamic central breaks (e4, d5) instead of slow manoeuvring. The Catalan's bishop on g2 is most powerful when the centre OPENS, not when it stays closed.
traps: If Black tries to hold the c4-pawn with …b5, you play a4 IMMEDIATELY — the Bg2 plus a4 undermining wrecks Black's queenside structure within 3-4 moves.
warnings: Don't play the Catalan on autopilot. Dubov's version requires SEEING the tactical possibilities at every move — if you play 'standard Catalan moves' without alertness, you'll miss the creative resources that make his version distinctive.
Watch Black's …c5 break. If timed correctly (after Black's pieces are fully developed), the …c5 break dissolves the central tension and the Bg2's diagonal becomes irrelevant.

variations.name: Open Catalan
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2 Be4 Qc1 Nc6
variations.explanation: Dubov's main Catalan with a creative twist on the standard plan. After 6...dxc4 7.Qc2 a6 8.Qxc4 b5 9.Qc2 Bb7 — Black sets up the queenside. Now 10.Bd2! (Dubov's choice — preparing Bc3 to challenge the long diagonal) Be4! (Black's counter-creative move) 11.Qc1 Nc6 — both sides reach an unbalanced position where dynamic chances dominate over positional. Pure Dubov chess.

variations.name: Closed Catalan with e4
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O Nbd7 Qc2 c6 Nbd2 b6 e4 Bb7 e5 Ne8 Re1 Nc7
variations.explanation: When Black plays the Closed Catalan (with 5...Nbd7 keeping d5 supported), Dubov plays AGGRESSIVELY with 9.e4! — pushing the central pawn even though it gives up Catalan flexibility. After 9...Bb7 10.e5 Ne8 (Black's knight is shoved back) 11.Re1 Nc7 — White has space and kingside attacking chances. Most Catalan players treat the Closed Catalan as a slow squeeze; Dubov treats it as a kingside-attack opening.

variations.name: Gambit Catalan
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 Be7 O-O O-O Ne5 Nc6 Bxc6 bxc6 Nxc6 Qe8 Nxe7+ Qxe7 Qa4 e5 dxe5 Qxe5
variations.explanation: Dubov's signature Catalan gambit. After 5...dxc4 6.Nf3 Be7 7.O-O O-O 8.Ne5! (the wild knight invasion) Nc6 9.Bxc6 bxc6 10.Nxc6 — White wins the bishop pair AND a tempo. After 10...Qe8 11.Nxe7+ Qxe7 12.Qa4 e5 13.dxe5 Qxe5 — the position is wildly unbalanced. Engines call it equal; over the board, the player who calculates better wins. Dubov has won spectacular games in this line.

openings.name: Anti-Marshall Ruy Lopez (Dubov)
openings.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nc6 Bb5 a6 Ba4 Nf6 O-O Be7 Re1 b5 Bb3 O-O h3 Bb7 d3 d6 a3 Na5 Ba2 c5 Nbd2 Nc6
